> In Direct Form I, the dashed noise arrows on the right all converge into the *main summer*, meaning **every** multiplier noise is filtered by the *entire* closed-loop transfer function $H(z)$. This is why DF-I has the factor $(2N+1)$ in its output noise variance.

## 4.3 Cascade Form — Noise Accumulation Through 2nd-Order Sections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA["x(n)"] --> sec1["Section 1\nH_1(z)"]
    sec1 -->|"+ 3 noise sources"| sec2["Section 2\nH_2(z)"]
    sec2 -->|"+ 3 noise sources"| sec3["Section K/2\nH_K/2(z)"]
    sec3 --> outA["y(n)"]
    sec1 -.->|"noise through H_2...H_K/2"| outA
    sec2 -.->|"noise through H_3...H_K/2"| outA
    sec3 -.->|"noise through no further filter"| outA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Cascade-form optimization principle (high-yield KTU concept):** Place the section with the **largest gain** *first* (closest to the input), because noise injected in early sections is attenuated by the gain of the remaining sections.

## 4.4 Limit-Cycle Behavior — First-Order IIR Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start["Initial y(-1) = y_0"] --> mulA["Multiply by a"]
    mulA --> roundQ["Round to nearest level"]
    roundQ -->|"y(n)"| outputA["Output Sample"]
    outputA --> delayA["Delay by 1 sample"]
    delayA --> mulA
    roundQ -.->|"Inject round-off e(n)"| outputA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The feedback loop on the left computes the next sample. The dashed arrow on the right shows the injected round-off error. In the *absence* of an input, this loop can sustain a non-decaying oscillation — the **limit cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

Define **product quantization error**. State the assumptions made in the statistical model of this error and derive its variance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Product quantization error is the difference between the *true* product of two finite-precision numbers and its *rounded* (or truncated) value stored in a finite register. It arises because the product of two $(b+1)$-bit numbers has up to $(2b+1)$ bits and must be quantized to $(b+1)$ bits to be fed back into the system.

> **Assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. $e(n)$ is **white** (uncorrelated from sample to sample).
> 2. $e(n)$ is **uniformly distributed** over $[-q/2, q/2]$.
> 3. $e(n)$ is **uncorrelated with the input** $x(n)$.
> 4. $e(n)$ is **stationary**.

> **Variance derivation (1 mark):** With $p(e) = 1/q$ for $e \in [-q/2, q/2]$:
> $$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-q/2}^{q/2} e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{q} \, de = \frac{1}{q} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-q/2}^{q/2} = \frac{q^2}{12}$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

What are **limit cycle oscillations** in recursive digital filters? Distinguish between **granular** and **overflow** limit cycles.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Limit cycle oscillations are sustained, non-decaying oscillations in the output of a recursive digital filter caused by the nonlinear effects of finite word-length arithmetic (round-off or overflow). They occur even when the input is identically zero or constant.

> **Distinction (1.5 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Granular Limit Cycle | Overflow Limit Cycle |
> |---|---|---|
> | Cause | Round-off in multiplication | Overflow in addition |
> | Amplitude | Small (a few quantization levels) | Large (close to $\pm V_{\max}$) |
> | Frequency | Depends on coefficient values | Usually very low (sign alternation) |
> | Mitigation | Use double-precision accumulators | Use saturation arithmetic / scaling |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** A second-order IIR filter has the difference equation:
$$y(n) = 1.5 \, y(n-1) - 0.7 \, y(n-2) + 0.4 \, x(n) + 0.2 \, x(n-1)$$

The filter is implemented in **Direct Form I** using fixed-point arithmetic with $b = 11$ fractional bits. Compute the **output round-off noise variance** $\sigma_f^2$. Assume the noise is white and uniformly distributed, and that every noise source passes through the same closed-loop transfer function $H(z)$.

Given:
$$\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega = 4.0$$

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **cascade form** realization of an IIR filter. State **two practical methods** to reduce round-off noise in cascade form, and justify each with a short reason.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify filter parameters (0.5 marks):**
- Order: $N = 2$
- Number of multipliers in DF-I: $L = 2N + 1 = 5$
- Fractional bits: $b = 11$

**Step 2 — Compute the quantization step size (1 mark):**
$$q = 2^{-b} = 2^{-11} = \frac{1}{2048} \approx 4.883 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the noise variance per source (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{(2^{-11})^2}{12} = \frac{2^{-22}}{12} = \frac{1}{12 \times 4194304} \approx 1.986 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Direct Form I formula (3 marks):**
$$\sigma_f^2 = (2N+1) \cdot \sigma_e^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 d\omega$$

Substituting the values:
$$\sigma_f^2 = 5 \times \frac{2^{-22}}{12} \times 4.0 = \frac{20 \times 2^{-22}}{12} = \frac{5}{3} \times 2^{-22}$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical value (1.5 marks):**
$$\sigma_f^2 = \frac{5}{3} \times 2.384 \times 10^{-7} \approx 3.97 \times 10^{-7}$$

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Cascade Form Definition (2 marks):**
In cascade form, the transfer function $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ is factored into a product of lower-order sections (typically 2nd-order):
$$H(z) = \prod_{i=1}^{K} H_i(z) = \prod_{i=1}^{K} \frac{b_{0i} + b_{1i} z^{-1} + b_{2i} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1i} z^{-1} + a_{2i} z^{-2}}$$

Each section is implemented using DF-I or DF-II, and the sections are connected in series.

**Method 1 — Optimal Pole-Zero Pairing (2.5 marks):**
Each 2nd-order section $H_i(z)$ has a pair of poles and a pair of zeros. The poles and zeros that are **geometrically closest** in the $z$-plane should be paired together into the same section. Justification: this minimizes the peak magnitude of each section's frequency response, hence reduces the amplification of round-off noise within the section.

**Method 2 — Optimal Section Ordering (2.5 marks):**
The sections should be ordered such that the section with the **largest gain** $\max_\omega \vert H_i(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is placed **first** (closest to the input). Justification: round-off noise injected in the first section is attenuated by the gain of all *subsequent* sections, so placing the noisiest section first minimizes the *output* noise variance.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the two methods as a *pair* (pairing + ordering) in your answer, since they are complementary.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **maximum amplitude** of the granular limit cycle for the first-order IIR filter:
$$y(n) = a \, y(n-1) + x(n)$$
implemented in fixed-point arithmetic with rounding, when the input $x(n) = 0$. Use the bound $\vert e(n) \vert \le q/2$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A first-order IIR filter has $a = 0.95$ and is implemented with $b = 8$ fractional bits. Compute:
1. The maximum amplitude of the granular limit cycle.
2. The number of quantization levels of oscillation.
3. The **dead-band input range** (i.e., the largest constant input magnitude for which the limit cycle can be sustained).

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Write the noise-injected recursion (1 mark):**
For $x(n) = 0$:
$$\hat{y}(n) = Q[a \, \hat{y}(n-1)] = a \, \hat{y}(n-1) + e(n)$$

**Step 2 — Express the output as a function of initial condition and accumulated errors (1.5 marks):**
Iterating the recursion:
$$\hat{y}(n) = a^n \, \hat{y}(-1) + \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a^{n-1-k} e(k)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the limit-cycle condition (1.5 marks):**
A limit cycle of period $P$ requires $\hat{y}(n+P) = \hat{y}(n)$. For a first-order filter, the only possible period is $P = 2$, with the output oscillating between $+y_m$ and $-y_m$.

**Step 4 — Apply the period-2 constraint (1.5 marks):**
For the sequence $\ldots, y_m, -y_m, y_m, -y_m, \ldots$:
$$y_m = a(-y_m) + e \implies y_m(1 + a) = e \implies y_m = \frac{e}{1 + a}$$
$$-y_m = a \, y_m + e' \implies -y_m(1 + a) = e' \implies -y_m = \frac{e'}{1 + a}$$

The **maximum** amplitude occurs when $\vert e \vert = \vert e' \vert = q/2$:
$$y_{m,\max} = \frac{q/2}{1 + a}$$

For $a > 0$ this is the upper bound; for $a < 0$ the bound is $\frac{q/2}{1 - \vert a \vert}$.

**Step 5 — Generalize (1.5 marks):**
$$\boxed{\vert y_m \vert \le \frac{q/2}{1 - \vert a \vert}}$$

#### Part (b) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (0.5 marks):**
$a = 0.95$, $b = 8 \implies q = 2^{-8} = 1/256 \approx 3.906 \times 10^{-3}$

**Step 2 — Compute max amplitude (2 marks):**
$$y_m = \frac{q/2}{1 - \vert a \vert} = \frac{1/512}{1 - 0.95} = \frac{1/512}{0.05} = \frac{1}{25.6} \approx 0.03906$$

**Step 3 — Express in quantization levels (2 marks):**
$$N_{\text{levels}} = \frac{y_m}{q} = \frac{0.03906}{1/256} = 0.03906 \times 256 = 10 \text{ levels}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the dead-band input range (2.5 marks):**
The dead-band is the largest constant input $x_0$ such that the output cannot decay to zero. For constant input $x_0$, the steady-state output (without quantization) is $y_\infty = x_0 / (1 - a)$. The limit cycle will mask the output whenever the steady-state is within the limit-cycle amplitude:
$$\left\vert \frac{x_0}{1 - a} \right\vert \le \frac{q/2}{1 - \vert a \vert}$$

For $a > 0$:
$$\vert x_0 \vert \le \frac{q}{2} = \frac{1}{512} \approx 1.953 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Max amplitude: $y_m \approx 0.0391$
- Number of levels: $10$ levels
- Dead-band input range: $\vert x_0 \vert \le 1.953 \times 10^{-3}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $q/2$ with $q$ in the limit-cycle formula** — many students write $q$ instead of $q/2$ and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **The dead-band formula depends on the sign of $a$** — for $a < 0$, the limit cycle is at $\frac{q/2}{1 + \vert a \vert}$, not $\frac{q/2}{1 - \vert a \vert}$. Always justify the denominator.
> 3. **In Part (a) of Question A**, students often forget the factor $(2N+1)$ and only compute $\sigma_e^2 \cdot \int \vert H \vert^2 d\omega$. The factor counts the **number of independent noise sources**.
> 4. **In the cascade form answer**, only stating "use cascade form" without naming *pairing* and *ordering* as separate methods loses 2–3 marks. Be explicit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Round-off error** = $Q[v] - v$, with $\vert e \vert \le q/2$ for rounding, where $q = 2^{-b}$.
- **Variance of round-off noise:** $\sigma_e^2 = q^2/12$. (Write this *first* in every noise-variance problem.)
- **Direct Form I output noise:** $\sigma_f^2 = (2N+1) \sigma_e^2 \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert H \vert^2 d\omega$. The factor is $2N+1$ because *every* multiplier noise passes through the *entire* filter.
- **Direct Form II** has fewer noise sources at the input side but is generally more noise-sensitive due to internal scaling.
- **Cascade form** is **always preferred** over DF-I/II for high-order filters because the noise can be controlled via **pole-zero pairing** and **section ordering**.
- **Parallel form** noise is similar to cascade, but sections are summed at the output — no noise from later sections passes through earlier ones.
- **FFT noise (radix-2):** $\sigma_f^2 = (N-1) q^2/12$ per output bin. The factor is $(N-1)$, **not** $N \log_2 N$.
- **Granular limit cycle (1st order):** $\vert y_m \vert \le \frac{q/2}{1 - \vert a \vert}$. The oscillation is between two levels.
- **Dead-band input range:** For 1st-order, $\vert x_0 \vert \le q/2$ (independent of $a$, for $a > 0$).
- **Overflow limit cycles** are large-amplitude and are mitigated by **saturation arithmetic** (clipping to $\pm V_{\max}$).
- **Statistical assumptions** (white, uniform, uncorrelated, stationary) must be **explicitly stated** in the answer for full marks.
- **For SNR problems:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sigma_y^2 / \sigma_f^2)$ — output signal power to output noise power.
- **Scaling** in fixed-point IIR filters: Multiply input by $A < 1$ to prevent overflow; this also reduces internal noise amplification.
- **Avoid direct forms for narrow-band, high-Q filters** — they amplify round-off noise dramatically. Use cascade or parallel form.
- **Block-floating-point FFTs** use a *single* exponent for an entire FFT block to balance dynamic range and noise performance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit Cycle Oscillations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Limit Cycle Oscillations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme / Discrete Time Signal Processing (Module 2)**, **Limit Cycle Oscillations** are defined as *unwanted, sustained, low-amplitude periodic oscillations* that appear at the output of a **recursive (IIR) digital filter** when the input is either **zero** (zero-input limit cycles) or **held constant** (constant-input limit cycles), purely as a consequence of **finite word-length effects** — specifically, **nonlinear quantisation** and **arithmetic overflow** in fixed-point implementations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCICT601 – Module 2):**
> Limit cycles are an *artefact* of nonlinear quantisation, NOT of the filter's actual linear transfer function. Theoretically stable filters (poles strictly inside the unit circle) can still produce audible/visible oscillations in hardware.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **swinging pendulum** in a watch. In an *ideal* (infinite-precision) world, after one big push, the swing would gradually decay to perfectly zero and stop. However, because the gears in the watch are manufactured with only a *limited* number of teeth (say, only 8 positions per revolution), the pendulum can never quite reach "exactly zero." Instead, it gets "stuck" in a tiny, repetitive back-and-forth wobble — a **limit cycle**.

In a digital IIR filter:
- The **coefficients** and **state variables** (delayed outputs) are stored using a *finite number of bits* (e.g., 8, 16, or 24 bits).
- Whenever a multiplication produces a value too large to fit, the system **overflows** or **rounds off**.
- This rounding "traps" the filter output in a small, repeating pattern — even when the input is set to **zero**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric for Quantisation Step:**
> $$Q = 2^{-(b-1)}$$
> where $b$ is the total number of bits in the fixed-point register. For a 4-bit two's complement number: $Q = 2^{-3} = 0.125$.

### 1.3 Two Principal Categories

| Category | Trigger Mechanism | Typical Period |
|---|---|---|
| **Overflow Limit Cycle** | Two's-complement wrap-around when product exceeds register range | Short period (often 1 or 2 samples) |
| **Granular (Round-off) Limit Cycle** | Rounding/Truncation of small-magnitude products | Variable; depends on the dead band |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** *Overflow* = the value "wraps around" the clock face. *Granular* = the value gets "stuck" between two fine gradations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

Consider a **first-order IIR filter** implemented with finite precision:

$$y(n) = Q\bigl[a\,y(n-1) + x(n)\bigr]$$

Step-by-step logic of how a limit cycle forms:

1. **Pole Location Is Stable in Theory** — Theoretically, $\vert a \vert < 1$ guarantees the impulse response decays to zero.
2. **Multiplication Produces Long Word-Length** — The product $a \cdot y(n-1)$ requires more bits than the register can hold.
3. **Quantisation $Q\{\cdot\}$ Is Nonlinear** — Rounding/truncation is a *nonlinear* operation; it does not scale linearly with input magnitude.
4. **Overflow (Saturation vs. Wrap-Around)** — In two's complement, exceeding the maximum positive value *wraps* to a large negative number, violating linearity.
5. **Feedback Through $y(n-1)$ Reinforces the Error** — The corrupted output becomes the next input, so the rounding error *propagates indefinitely*.
6. **Output Becomes Trapped in a Finite State Space** — With only $2^b$ possible output values, the system must eventually revisit a previous state, producing a *periodic* trajectory — the **limit cycle**.

### 2.2 Zero-Input Limit Cycle (Overflow-Driven)

With $x(n) = 0$, the recursion simplifies to:

$$y(n) = Q\bigl[a\,y(n-1)\bigr]$$

If the quantiser is **two's-complement wrap-around**, and the product $a \cdot y(n-1)$ would exceed the representable range, the output **wraps** to a large magnitude *of opposite sign*. The recursive nature then sustains the oscillation between the two extreme values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> If the multiplier $a$ is *negative* and the system is in two's-complement arithmetic, a *single overflow event* is sufficient to throw the filter into a persistent $\pm$M oscillation, where $M$ is the maximum positive representable value.

### 2.3 Granular Limit Cycle (Round-off-Driven)

Here, the issue is **not** overflow but the *rounding* of small-magnitude products to the nearest quantization level. For a first-order filter with rounding, the output becomes periodic in the range:

$$-\frac{Q}{1-\vert a \vert} \;\le\; y(n) \;\le\; \frac{Q}{1-\vert a \vert}$$

This range is called the **dead band** of the filter.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are tested in KTU University Examinations (PCICT601).**

| Symbol / Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|
| $y(n) = Q[a\,y(n-1) + x(n)]$ | General 1st-order IIR filter with quantisation |
| $Q = 2^{-(b-1)}$ | Quantization step for $b$-bit two's complement |
| $\vert a \vert < 1$ | Linear stability condition (poles inside unit circle) |
| Dead band: $\dfrac{Q}{1-\vert a \vert}$ | Maximum amplitude of granular limit cycle (rounding) |
| Dead band (magnitude truncation): $\dfrac{Q}{1-\vert a \vert} \cdot \vert a \vert$ | Smaller dead band (rounding preferred) |
| $\vert a \vert \le 1 - 2^{-b}$ | Condition to *avoid* overflow oscillations in 2nd-order sections |
| Saturation: $y(n) = \mathrm{sat}\{a\,y(n-1) + x(n)\}$ | Overflow prevention (clamps to $\pm M$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table-Syntax Note:** The vertical bar for absolute value is rendered using `\vert` (not `\|`) to preserve the markdown table layout.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC):** Limit cycles cause *audible hissing/buzzing* in low-bit-rate playback. Designers use higher word-lengths or noise-shaping.
- **Digital Hearing Aids:** Even sub-milliwatt limit cycles are audible and annoying; saturation arithmetic is mandatory.
- **Control Systems (DSP-based PID controllers):** Limit cycles cause *position dithering* in servo motors.
- **Biomedical Implants (Pacemakers, Cochlear Implants):** Zero tolerance for sustained oscillations — *saturation overflow* and *double-precision accumulators* are standard practice.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Granular Limit Cycle of a First-Order IIR Filter

**Given Problem Statement (Worked Example):**
A first-order IIR filter is described by $y(n) = a\,y(n-1) + x(n)$ with $a = -0.875$.
The filter is implemented using **4-bit two's-complement fixed-point arithmetic** with rounding.
Compute the output for $n \geq 0$ assuming $x(n) = 0$ and $y(-1) = 7$.

**Step 1 — Determine the quantisation step $Q$.**
For a 4-bit two's-complement register, the smallest representable increment is one LSB. The range is $-8$ to $+7$, so the step is $Q = 1$.

**Step 2 — Establish the recursion at $x(n) = 0$.**
With $x(n) = 0$, the recursion reduces to:
$$y(n) = Q\bigl[a\,y(n-1)\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot y(n-1)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the first sample $y(0)$.**
$$y(0) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot 7\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-6.125\bigr] = -6$$
Logic: $-6.125$ lies between $-7$ and $-6$; rounding to the nearest integer gives $-6$.

**Step 4 — Compute $y(1)$.**
$$y(1) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot (-6)\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[5.25\bigr] = 5$$
Logic: $5.25$ is closer to $5$ than to $6$.

**Step 5 — Compute $y(2)$.**
$$y(2) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot 5\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-4.375\bigr] = -4$$

**Step 6 — Compute $y(3)$.**
$$y(3) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot (-4)\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[3.5\bigr] = 4$$
Logic: $3.5$ is exactly midway between $3$ and $4$. By the *round-half-up* convention, it rounds to $4$.

**Step 7 — Compute $y(4)$.**
$$y(4) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot 4\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-3.5\bigr] = -4$$

**Step 8 — Compute $y(5)$ and detect the limit cycle.**
$$y(5) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[-0.875 \cdot (-4)\bigr] = \mathrm{round}\bigl[3.5\bigr] = 4$$

We now observe $y(4) = -4$ and $y(5) = 4$, and the next step will return $-4$ again. **The output has entered a period-2 limit cycle of amplitude $\pm 4$.**

**Step 9 — Tabulate the complete trajectory.**

| $n$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ | $6$ | $\ldots$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y(n)$ | $7$ | $-6$ | $5$ | $-4$ | $4$ | $-4$ | $4$ | $-4$ | $\ldots$ |

**Step 10 — Verify against the theoretical dead-band formula.**
The theoretical maximum amplitude of a granular limit cycle is:

$$\frac{Q}{1-\vert a \vert} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.875} = \frac{1}{0.125} = 8$$

The observed amplitude $4$ lies *within* this dead band, confirming consistency with theory.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dead-Band Boundary for a First-Order Filter (Rounding)

We want to find the range of $y(n)$ for which subsequent samples remain bounded within the same interval (i.e., the system has reached its "dead zone").

Consider the condition that the output remains in the same quantisation cell:
$$\vert y(n) - y(n-1) \vert < \frac{Q}{2}$$

For a 1st-order filter with $x(n) = 0$ and rounding:
$$y(n) = a\,y(n-1) + e(n)$$
where $e(n)$ is the quantisation error with $\vert e(n) \vert \leq Q/2$. Then:

$$y(n) - y(n-1) = (a-1)\,y(n-1) + e(n)$$

For the system to remain in the same cell, the *worst-case* error propagation gives:
$$\vert (a-1)\,y(n-1) \vert + \vert e(n) \vert < \frac{Q}{2}$$

Substituting the upper bound $\vert e(n) \vert = Q/2$:
$$(1 - \vert a \vert)\,\vert y(n-1) \vert < Q$$

Solving for $\vert y(n-1) \vert$:
$$\vert y(n-1) \vert < \frac{Q}{1 - \vert a \vert}$$

Hence the **dead-band amplitude** is:

$$\boxed{\,y_{\text{dead}} = \pm \frac{Q}{1 - \vert a \vert}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:** This derivation is *frequently* asked as a 7-mark sub-question. Memorise the *three* logical lines: (1) difference equation, (2) error bound substitution, (3) final isolation of $\vert y \vert$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Prevention Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Eliminates | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Saturation Arithmetic** | Clamp overflow to $\pm M$ | Overflow limit cycle | Slight nonlinear distortion at extremes |
| **Round-off (vs. Truncation)** | Round half-up instead of chopping | Reduces granular amplitude | Requires extra hardware for LSB logic |
| **Magnitude Truncation** | Always round toward zero | Granular limit cycle | Slightly larger dead band than proper rounding |
| **Increase Word-Length** | Use 24-bit instead of 16-bit | All limit cycles | Higher cost, power, latency |
| **Cascade Realisation** | Pair poles/zeros carefully | Overflow sensitivity | Design effort |
| **Double-Precision Accumulator** | Keep accumulator in 32/64-bit | Granular limit cycle | Slower; needs careful scaling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: IIR Filter With Quantisation Showing Limit-Cycle Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input x of n"] --> B["Adder Node"]
    C["Quantizer Q dot"] --> B
    B --> D["Multiplier a times y n minus 1"]
    D --> E["Storage Register y n minus 1"]
    E --> F["Output y of n"]
    F --> G["Quantization Error e of n"]
    G --> C
    H["Overflow Detector"] --> I["Saturation Logic"]
    B --> H
    I --> F
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0
    style F fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100
    style I fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style H fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F
```

**Interpretation of the Flowchart:**

1. The **Input x(n)** enters the **Adder Node** together with feedback from the quantiser.
2. The **Multiplier** scales $y(n-1)$ by coefficient $a$.
3. The product is stored in a finite-precision **Storage Register** — this is where the quantisation error $e(n)$ is generated.
4. The error is fed back into the adder, creating a *nonlinear* closed loop.
5. The **Overflow Detector** watches the adder output. If overflow is detected, **Saturation Logic** clamps the value to the maximum representable magnitude, *preventing* the wrap-around that causes overflow limit cycles.
6. The corrected output $y(n)$ emerges at the right, ready for the next iteration.

### 4.2 State-Transition Diagram: Granular Limit Cycle Visualisation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialCondition
    InitialCondition: y of minus 1 equals 7
    InitialCondition --> NodeS6: y of 0 equals minus 6
    NodeS6: y equals minus 6
    NodeS6 --> NodeS5: y of 1 equals 5
    NodeS5: y equals 5
    NodeS5 --> NodeS4: y of 2 equals minus 4
    NodeS4: y equals minus 4
    NodeS4 --> NodeP4: y of 3 equals 4
    NodeP4: y equals 4
    NodeP4 --> NodeS4: y of 4 equals minus 4
    NodeS4 --> NodeP4: y of 5 equals 4
    NodeP4 --> [*]: Limit cycle locked
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram visualises the **state trajectory** computed in Section 3.1. The system starts at $y(-1) = 7$, walks through transient states, and then **locks into a closed loop** between $-4$ and $+4$ — the signature of a **period-2 granular limit cycle**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define limit cycle oscillations in recursive digital filters. Why do they occur even when the filter is theoretically stable?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Limit cycle oscillations are unwanted, sustained periodic oscillations that appear at the output of a recursive (IIR) digital filter implemented with finite word-length arithmetic, even when the input is set to zero.
**Cause (1 mark):** They occur because the *nonlinear quantisation* (rounding/truncation) and *two's-complement overflow* trap the output in a finite state space, producing periodic behaviour.
**Stability Paradox (1 mark):** Theoretically, a stable filter ($\vert a \vert < 1$) has its impulse response decay to zero in *infinite* precision. However, finite precision destroys linearity, so the system can no longer be described by the linear difference equation — and may sustain oscillations that are *artefacts of the quantiser*, not of the filter's poles.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between overflow limit cycles and granular limit cycles.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Overflow limit cycles (1.5 marks):** Caused by *two's-complement wrap-around* when the product $a \cdot y(n-1)$ exceeds the maximum representable positive or negative value. The output oscillates between the extreme representable values ($\pm M$). Eliminated by **saturation arithmetic**.
**Granular limit cycles (1.5 marks):** Caused by *rounding/truncation* of small-magnitude products to the nearest quantisation level. The output is trapped in a *dead band* of amplitude $Q/(1-\vert a \vert)$ for rounding. Reduced by using *magnitude truncation* or *higher word-length*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is allowed between two questions. Students must answer **one full question** of their choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) Explain zero-input limit cycle oscillations in IIR digital filters due to overflow non-linearity. Show with a suitable example how a single overflow event sustains a persistent oscillation.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup the recursion (1 mark):**
Consider a first-order IIR filter implemented in two's-complement arithmetic:
$$y(n) = Q\bigl[a \cdot y(n-1)\bigr]$$
With $a = -1$ for the simplest case, in 4-bit two's complement with range $-8$ to $+7$.

**Step 2 — Define the overflow mechanism (2 marks):**
In two's complement, the maximum representable value is $M = 2^{b-1} - 1 = 7$ for $b=4$. If the true product exceeds $+7$, the bits *wrap around* to a large negative number. For example, $-1 \times 7 = -7$ is fine, but if the computation produces $+8$, the 4-bit register stores it as $-8$.

**Step 3 — Worked example (3 marks):**
Let $y(-1) = +7$. The system is supposed to decay, but the product alternates sign:
- $y(0) = Q[-1 \times 7] = Q[-7] = -7$ (within range)
- $y(1) = Q[-1 \times -7] = Q[+7] = +7$
- $y(2) = Q[-1 \times 7] = -7$
- $\vdots$

The output *oscillates* between $+7$ and $-7$ **forever**, even with zero input. The amplitude $M$ is the maximum possible — this is a *severe* overflow limit cycle.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
This demonstrates that even a *single* overflow event in two's-complement wrap-around can produce a sustained large-amplitude oscillation. The remedy is **saturation arithmetic**: clamp any value exceeding $\pm M$ to $\pm M$ itself.

---

**(b) A first-order IIR filter is described by $y(n) = 0.9375 \cdot y(n-1) + x(n)$ and is implemented using 4-bit two's-complement arithmetic with rounding. The input is $x(n) = 0$ for all $n$, and the initial condition is $y(-1) = 4$. Compute and tabulate $y(n)$ for $n = 0$ to $n = 6$ and identify any limit cycle.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (0.5 marks):**
$b = 4$ bits, $Q = 1$, $a = 0.9375$, $x(n) = 0$, $y(-1) = 4$.

**Step 2 — Recursion (0.5 marks):**
$$y(n) = \mathrm{round}\bigl[0.9375 \cdot y(n-1)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Sequential computation (5 marks):**

$$y(0) = \mathrm{round}[0.9375 \times 4] = \mathrm{round}[3.75] = 4$$

$$y(1) = \mathrm{round}[0.9375 \times 4] = \mathrm{round}[3.75] = 4$$

The system has immediately entered a **period-1 limit cycle** at $y = 4$.

**Step 4 — Tabulation (0.5 marks):**

| $n$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ | $4$ | $5$ | $6$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y(n)$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ | $4$ |

**Step 5 — Verify with dead-band formula (0.5 marks):**
$$y_{\text{dead}} = \frac{Q}{1 - \vert a \vert} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.9375} = \frac{1}{0.0625} = 16$$
Since $\vert 4 \vert < 16$, the value $y = 4$ is *within* the dead band, confirming the limit cycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the phenomenon of granular limit cycle oscillations in recursive digital filters.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Granular limit cycles are low-amplitude sustained oscillations produced by *rounding* (or truncation) of small-magnitude products in a finite-word-length filter.

**Step 2 — Diagram (2 marks):**
The block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes should be reproduced, showing: input → adder → multiplier → quantiser → delay → output, with the quantisation error $e(n)$ fed back into the adder.

**Step 3 — Mechanism (2 marks):**
When $\vert y(n-1) \vert$ is small, the product $a \cdot y(n-1)$ may round to a value that, in the next iteration, produces *the same* quantised output. The system reaches a fixed point in the quantised state space and *oscillates* around it.

**Step 4 — Dead band (2 marks):**
The amplitude of the oscillation is bounded by:
$$\vert y(n) \vert \le \frac{Q}{1 - \vert a \vert}$$
Outside this dead band, the linear dynamics dominate and the output decays; *inside* the dead band, the system is locked.

---

**(b) Derive the dead-band condition for a first-order IIR filter $y(n) = a\,y(n-1) + x(n)$ implemented with rounding quantisation. State the formula and its significance.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the quantised difference equation (1 mark):**
$$y(n) = a\,y(n-1) + x(n) + e(n)$$
where $\vert e(n) \vert \leq Q/2$ is the rounding error.

**Step 2 — Form the inter-sample difference (1 mark):**
$$y(n) - y(n-1) = (a-1)\,y(n-1) + e(n)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the worst-case bound (2 marks):**
For the output to remain in the same quantisation cell:
$$\vert y(n) - y(n-1) \vert < \frac{Q}{2}$$
Using the triangle inequality:
$$\vert a-1 \vert \cdot \vert y(n-1) \vert + \vert e(n) \vert < \frac{Q}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the bound on $e(n)$ (1 mark):**
$$(1 - \vert a \vert)\,\vert y(n-1) \vert < \frac{Q}{2} + \frac{Q}{2} = Q$$

**Step 5 — Final formula and significance (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{\,\vert y(n) \vert < \frac{Q}{1 - \vert a \vert}\,}$$
**Significance:** This *dead band* defines the range of output amplitudes where granular limit cycles can occur. Practical filters should either operate outside this range, use higher word-length (smaller $Q$), or apply *magnitude truncation* to keep $a$ small.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Direct from Board Marking Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply rounding vs. truncation rules** — many students truncate instead of round, producing wrong numerical answers. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation of the dead-band formula** — Part-B questions often demand a *derivation*, not just a statement. A formula stated without justification gets only **1 of 7 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing "stable filter" with "no limit cycle"** — limit cycles are a *nonlinear* effect; emphasise that *theoretical stability* does NOT prevent them in finite precision.
> 4. **Wrong sign convention in two's complement** — remember that the most negative value is $-2^{b-1}$ and the most positive is $+2^{b-1} - 1$ (asymmetric range). Conflating these loses **1 mark** in computation.
> 5. **Not specifying the period of the limit cycle** — always state whether it is period-1 (single value), period-2, or higher.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Limit Cycle = Nonlinear Artefact:** Not due to filter poles; caused by quantisation + overflow.
- **Two flavours:** *Overflow* (driven by wrap-around, large amplitude $\pm M$) and *Granular* (driven by rounding, small amplitude).
- **Dead-Band Formula (Rounding):** $\dfrac{Q}{1 - \vert a \vert}$ — **must be memorised** for KTU ESE.
- **Dead-Band Formula (Magnitude Truncation):** $\dfrac{\vert a \vert \cdot Q}{1 - \vert a \vert}$ — smaller, hence preferred.
- **Overflow Prevention:** **Saturation arithmetic** (clamp to $\pm M$).
- **Granular Prevention:** Round-off (not truncation) + larger $b$ (more bits).
- **Worked Numerical Example Recap:** $a = -0.875$, 4-bit, $y(-1) = 7$ $\Rightarrow$ period-2 limit cycle of amplitude $\pm 4$.
- **Stability Paradox:** $\vert a \vert < 1$ guarantees linear stability, **not** nonlinear limit-cycle-free behaviour.
- **Period-1 vs. Period-2:** Period-1 is a constant DC offset; period-2 alternates between two values.
- **Cascading sections** is preferable to direct-form for limiting overflow sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IIR Digital Filters: Digital Filter Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** IIR Digital Filters: Digital Filter Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of IIR Digital Filters

An **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant system whose impulse response $h[n]$ extends to infinity in duration. Mathematically, it is described by a constant-coefficient linear difference equation of the form:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

where $a_k$ and $b_k$ are the filter coefficients, $x[n]$ is the input, and $y[n]$ is the output. The presence of feedback (the $y[n-k]$ terms with $k \geq 1$) is what gives the filter its "infinite" memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> An IIR filter is a recursive digital filter characterized by a rational system function $H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$, where both numerator and denominator are polynomials in $z^{-1}$, and $A(z) \neq 1$ (i.e., poles are present).

### 1.2 Digital Filter Specifications — Definition

**Digital filter specifications** are the precise set of magnitude and/or phase requirements that a designed filter must satisfy in the frequency domain. They act as the *contract* between the design engineer and the physical implementation.

The four mandatory parameters that fully specify a lowpass IIR digital filter are:

1. **Passband edge frequency** $\omega_p$ (in rad/sample)
2. **Stopband edge frequency** $\omega_s$ (in rad/sample)
3. **Passband ripple** $\delta_p$ (or $A_p$ in dB)
4. **Stopband ripple** $\delta_s$ (or $A_s$ in dB)

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water Faucet with a Tolerance Band:**
> Imagine designing a household water filter. The user says: *"Let pure water flow between 0 and 5 liters/min exactly as it is, but at 7 liters/min and beyond, block at least 99% of impurities."* You cannot build a *brick wall* filter — there is a transition region (5 to 7 liters/min) where filtering gradually increases. The four specifications are your **engineering contract** with the user: *which frequencies pass, which are blocked, and how imperfect the passing and blocking are allowed to be.*

In signal processing, the *frequencies* are what matters, and the imperfections are called **ripples** (small wiggles in the passband) and **attenuation** (incomplete rejection in the stopband).

### 1.4 Classification of Filters by Frequency Response

| Filter Type | Passband | Stopband |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowpass (LPF)** | $0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_p$ | $\omega_s \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$ |
| **Highpass (HPF)** | $\omega_p \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$ | $0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_s$ |
| **Bandpass (BPF)** | $\omega_{p1} \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_{p2}$ | $0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_{s1}$ and $\omega_{s2} \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$ |
| **Bandstop (BSF)** | $0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_{p1}$ and $\omega_{p2} \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$ | $\omega_{s1} \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_{s2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Magnitude Specification — Tolerance Scheme

The magnitude response of a *practical* digital filter is not a rectangular brick-wall shape. Instead, it is constrained to lie within a **tolerance band** around the ideal magnitude (which is 1 in the passband and 0 in the stopband).

For a **lowpass IIR filter**, the constraints are:

$$
1 - \delta_p \;\le\; \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \;\le\; 1, \quad \text{for } 0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_p
$$

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \;\le\; \delta_s, \quad \text{for } \omega_s \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi
$$

The region $\omega_p < \vert \omega \vert < \omega_s$ is the **transition band** — the filter response is unspecified (free) here.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Equivalent Ripple Representations:**
> - **Linear (absolute) ripple:** $\delta_p$, $\delta_s$
> - **Logarithmic (dB) ripple:** $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p)$ dB and $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ dB
>
> KTU board questions often state specs in **dB** and expect the answer back in dB.

### 2.2 Why IIR and Not FIR for These Specs?

A KTU-favorite conceptual point: when specifications require a **narrow transition band** (small $(\omega_s - \omega_p)$) and **high stopband attenuation**, IIR filters are preferred because they can achieve the same performance with **far fewer coefficients** than FIR filters, owing to their pole-zero structure (poles give sharper roll-off).

### 2.3 The IIR Design Problem Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Formulation:**
> *Given the four parameters $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$, $\delta_p$ (or $A_p$), $\delta_s$ (or $A_s$), design a causal stable IIR digital filter $H(z)$ whose magnitude response meets these tolerances.*

The two main solution routes studied in KTU Module 3 are:
1. **Design from analog prototype** (Butterworth, Chebyshev-I, Chebyshev-II, Elliptic) — converted via **Impulse Invariance** or **Bilinear Transformation**.
2. **Direct digital design** (less common at KTU level).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|
| Passband magnitude bound | $1 - \delta_p \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1$ | unitless |
| Stopband magnitude bound | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \delta_s$ | unitless |
| Passband ripple (dB) | $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p)$ | dB |
| Stopband attenuation (dB) | $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ | dB |
| Inverse: $\delta_p$ from $A_p$ | $\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}$ | unitless |
| Inverse: $\delta_s$ from $A_s$ | $\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20}$ | unitless |
| Pre-warping for BLT | $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | rad/sec |
| Discrete-to-Analog freq mapping | $\omega = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)$ | rad/sample |
| Butterworth order $N$ | $N \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)}{2\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$ | integer |
| Chebyshev-I order $N$ | $N \ge \dfrac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}}\right)}{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$ | integer |
| Analog LPF cutoff (Butterworth) | $\Omega_c = \dfrac{\Omega_p}{\left(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1\right)^{1/(2N)}}$ | rad/sec |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio engineering:** Lowpass filter to remove high-frequency hiss ($\omega_s \approx$ 16 kHz for CD quality).
- **Biomedical ECG monitors:** Bandpass filter (0.5–40 Hz) to isolate heart signals from noise.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel-selecting bandpass filters in software-defined radio.
- **Anti-aliasing:** Pre-ADC analog filter matched to digital specs.
- **Control systems:** Notch filters at 50/60 Hz for power-line hum rejection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Magnitude Specification for a Real Lowpass IIR Filter

A causal IIR filter has system function:

$$
H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}
$$

On the unit circle ($z = e^{j\omega}$), the magnitude response is:

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{B(e^{j\omega})}{A(e^{j\omega})} \right\vert = \frac{\vert B(e^{j\omega}) \vert}{\vert A(e^{j\omega}) \vert}
$$

**Step 1:** In the passband, we require the magnitude to be as close to **unity** (the ideal) as possible, with a maximum permissible deviation of $\delta_p$. Hence:

$$
1 - \delta_p \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1
$$

**Step 2:** In the stopband, the magnitude must be reduced to nearly zero, with the maximum allowed leakage being $\delta_s$:

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \delta_s
$$

**Step 3:** Expressing in **decibels** (logarithmic scale) for human-friendly engineering:

$$
20\log_{10}\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p) = -A_p \text{ dB} \quad \text{(passband)}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 20\log_{10}(\delta_s) = -A_s \text{ dB} \quad \text{(stopband)}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for ripple parameters from dB specs:

$$
\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p / 20}
$$

$$
\delta_s = 10^{-A_s / 20}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dB?** Because the human ear and most communication receivers perceive signal power logarithmically. A 60 dB attenuation means the unwanted signal is reduced to 1-millionth of its input power — a specification that becomes trivially easy to express in dB.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — Setting Up the Tolerance Scheme

**Problem (KTU Model):** *For an IIR lowpass filter, the specifications are: $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample, $\omega_s = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample, $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 40$ dB. Compute $\delta_p$, $\delta_s$ and write the magnitude constraints.*

**Step 1 — Compute $\delta_p$:**

$$
\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20} = 1 - 10^{-1/20} = 1 - 10^{-0.05}
$$

$$
10^{-0.05} = \frac{1}{10^{0.05}} = \frac{1}{1.1220} \approx 0.8913
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_p \approx 1 - 0.8913 = 0.1087}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\delta_s$:**

$$
\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20} = 10^{-40/20} = 10^{-2} = 0.01
$$

**Step 3 — Write the magnitude specification constraints:**

$$
0.8913 \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1, \quad 0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le 0.2\pi
$$

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 0.01, \quad 0.4\pi \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi
$$

### 3.3 Pre-warping Derivation (for Bilinear Transformation)

The Bilinear Transformation (BLT) is the most common analog-to-digital mapping in KTU Module 3. It introduces **frequency warping** (non-linear compression), which must be corrected using pre-warping.

**Step 1 — Start with the BLT mapping:**

$$
s = \frac{2}{T}\!\left(\frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}\right) \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; z = \frac{1 + (sT/2)}{1 - (sT/2)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ and $s = j\Omega$:**

$$
j\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\!\left(\frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}}{1 + e^{-j\omega}}\right) = \frac{2}{T}\!\left(\frac{e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}}{e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}}\right)\frac{e^{j\omega/2}}{e^{j\omega/2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's identities** $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$ and $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos\theta$:

$$
j\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\!\left(\frac{2j\sin(\omega/2)}{2\cos(\omega/2)}\right) = j\,\frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Final pre-warping formula:**

$$
\boxed{\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — Inverse (for analog-to-digital mapping):**

$$
\omega = 2\arctan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Step in KTU Problems:**
> Before applying BLT, transform the *digital* critical frequencies to *analog* using pre-warping. This guarantees the final digital filter meets the specs **exactly** at $\omega_p$ and $\omega_s$ — without this, the realized filter would fail specs near the Nyquist limit.

### 3.4 Order Computation for Butterworth IIR LPF (Full Derivation)

The Butterworth magnitude-squared response of order $N$ and cutoff $\Omega_c$ is:

$$
\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply passband condition at $\Omega = \Omega_p$:**

$$
\frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}} \ge (1 - \delta_p)^2 = 10^{-A_p/10}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply stopband condition at $\Omega = \Omega_s$:**

$$
\frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}} \le \delta_s^2 = 10^{-A_s/10}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange both inequalities** to isolate the $(\Omega / \Omega_c)^{2N}$ term:

$$
\left(\frac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} \le 10^{0.1 A_p} - 1
$$

$$
\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} \ge 10^{0.1 A_s} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Take the ratio** of the two inequalities (eliminating $\Omega_c$):

$$
\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)^{2N} \ge \frac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Take $\log_{10}$ and solve for $N$:**

$$
N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)}{2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}
$$

The integer ceiling of this expression is the **minimum filter order** required to meet specs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Tolerance Scheme for Lowpass IIR Filter

This diagram visualizes the magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ of a lowpass IIR filter as a function of the normalized frequency $\omega$ (ranging from $0$ to $\pi$). The shaded regions indicate the **tolerance bands** allowed by the specification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axis[Frequency axis: omega from 0 to pi]:::axisStyle
    pb[Passband region: 0 to omega_p]:::passStyle
    tb[Transition band: omega_p to omega_s]:::transStyle
    sb[Stopband region: omega_s to pi]:::stopStyle
    ub[Upper bound: magnitude equals 1]:::boundStyle
    lbpb[Lower passband bound: 1 minus delta_p]:::boundStyle
    lbsb[Stopband bound: delta_s]:::boundStyle

    axis --> pb
    axis --> tb
    axis --> sb
    pb --> ub
    pb --> lbpb
    sb --> lbsb

    classDef axisStyle fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef passStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#003366
    classDef transStyle fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:1px,color:#806600
    classDef stopStyle fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#800000,stroke-width:1px,color:#800000
    classDef boundStyle fill:#e6f5e6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px,color:#006600
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — IIR Filter Design Flow (From Specs to $H(z)$)

This diagram shows the canonical design pipeline that KTU Module 3 expects students to be able to draw and explain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    specA[Input Specifications: omega_p, omega_s, A_p, A_s]:::stepA
    prewrapA[Pre-warp to Analog Frequencies: Omega_p, Omega_s]:::stepA
    orderA[Compute Minimum Order N from Specs]:::stepA
    protoA[Choose Analog Prototype: Butterworth OR Chebyshev OR Elliptic]:::stepA
    analogA[Determine Analog H_a(s)]:::stepA
    convA[Convert to Digital via BLT OR Impulse Invariance]:::stepA
    outA[Final IIR Filter: H z]:::stepA

    specA --> prewrapA --> orderA --> protoA --> analogA --> convA --> outA

    classDef stepA fill:#dde7ff,stroke:#1a3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#1a3a8a
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an IIR Filter Implementation

A direct-form-II (canonical) realization of an IIR difference equation, which is the target structure after design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Input x of n]:::nodeA
    delayA1[z power minus 1]:::nodeA
    delayA2[z power minus 1]:::nodeA
    delayA3[z power minus 1]:::nodeA
    sumA[Adder Node]:::nodeA
    bcoeffA1[b zero]:::nodeA
    bcoeffA2[b one]:::nodeA
    bcoeffA3[b two]:::nodeA
    acoeffA1[a one with sign]:::nodeA
    acoeffA2[a two with sign]:::nodeA
    outA[Output y of n]:::nodeA

    inpA --> bcoeffA1 --> sumA
    bcoeffA2 --> sumA
    bcoeffA3 --> sumA
    sumA --> delayA1
    delayA1 --> delayA2
    delayA2 --> delayA3
    delayA3 --> acoeffA1
    delayA3 --> acoeffA2
    acoeffA1 --> sumA
    acoeffA2 --> sumA
    sumA --> outA

    classDef nodeA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:1px,color:#806000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The forward path applies the **FIR (numerator)** coefficients $b_0, b_1, b_2, \ldots$ to a delayed version of $x[n]$, while the feedback path applies the **IIR (denominator)** coefficients $-a_1, -a_2, \ldots$ to delayed $y[n]$. The presence of feedback is what makes the response infinite in duration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define digital filter specifications. List the four parameters required to completely specify a lowpass digital IIR filter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Digital filter specifications are the set of magnitude and/or phase response constraints in the frequency domain that the designed filter must satisfy. For a lowpass IIR digital filter, the four required parameters are:
1. **Passband edge frequency** $\omega_p$ — the maximum frequency that must pass with small distortion.
2. **Stopband edge frequency** $\omega_s$ — the minimum frequency that must be heavily attenuated.
3. **Passband ripple** $\delta_p$ (or $A_p$ in dB) — the maximum permissible deviation from unity gain in the passband.
4. **Stopband attenuation** $\delta_s$ (or $A_s$ in dB) — the maximum permissible magnitude in the stopband.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Distinguish between passband ripple $\delta_p$ and stopband ripple $\delta_s$ in an IIR filter specification. Why are specifications often expressed in decibels?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **$\delta_p$** is the maximum deviation of $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ from unity in the passband — i.e., how *imperfectly* the filter passes desired frequencies.
- **$\delta_s$** is the maximum allowed magnitude of $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ in the stopband — i.e., how *imperfectly* the filter blocks unwanted frequencies.
- They differ by several orders of magnitude (e.g., $\delta_p = 0.1087$ for 1 dB ripple, but $\delta_s = 0.01$ for 40 dB attenuation). Expressing in decibels converts this wide range into manageable, additive quantities: $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p)$ and $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$. Decibels also align with the logarithmic perception of signal power in audio and communications.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Style]*

**(a)** *Explain the magnitude specifications of an IIR lowpass digital filter with the help of a neat tolerance scheme diagram. Derive the relationship between passband ripple $\delta_p$ and passband ripple in dB, $A_p$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The magnitude specifications of a lowpass IIR digital filter are defined by a *tolerance band* on the magnitude response. The passband extends from $\omega = 0$ to $\omega = \omega_p$, and the stopband from $\omega = \omega_s$ to $\omega = \pi$, with a transition band in between.

**Magnitude constraints:**

$$
1 - \delta_p \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1, \quad 0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_p
$$

$$
\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \delta_s, \quad \omega_s \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi
$$

**Tolerance Scheme (verbal description for the exam):** Plot $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ on the y-axis and $\omega$ on the x-axis. The passband is a horizontal band of width $\delta_p$ near magnitude 1. The stopband is a horizontal line at $\delta_s$ (a small value). The transition band is a monotonically decreasing curve connecting them.

**Derivation of $\delta_p$ vs $A_p$:**

The maximum deviation from unity in the passband is $(1 - \delta_p)$ on a linear scale. In dB, this is:

$$
20\log_{10}(1 - \delta_p) = -A_p \text{ dB}
$$

Taking $10^{x/20}$ on both sides:

$$
1 - \delta_p = 10^{-A_p/20}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}}
$$

**(b)** *A digital IIR lowpass filter is to be designed with specifications: $\omega_p = 0.25\pi$ rad/sample, $\omega_s = 0.45\pi$ rad/sample, $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 30$ dB. Determine the minimum order $N$ of a Butterworth filter (assuming sampling period $T = 1$ sec) using the Bilinear Transformation. State the pre-warped analog frequencies.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the digital critical frequencies to analog:**

$$
\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{0.25\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.125\pi) = 2 \times 0.4142 = 0.8284 \text{ rad/sec}
$$

$$
\Omega_s = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right) = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{0.45\pi}{2}\right) = 2\tan(0.225\pi) = 2 \times 0.8819 = 1.7638 \text{ rad/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Butterworth order formula:**

$$
N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 \times 30} - 1}{10^{0.1 \times 1} - 1}\right)}{2\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.7638}{0.8284}\right)}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
10^{3} - 1 = 999, \quad 10^{0.1} - 1 = 1.2589 - 1 = 0.2589
$$

$$
\frac{999}{0.2589} \approx 3858.3 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \log_{10}(3858.3) \approx 3.5864
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
\frac{1.7638}{0.8284} \approx 2.1292 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2 \log_{10}(2.1292) \approx 2 \times 0.3283 = 0.6566
$$

**Step 3 — Compute and round up:**

$$
N \ge \frac{3.5864}{0.6566} \approx 5.46
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 6}
$$

The minimum required Butterworth filter order is 6.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]*

**(a)** *Compare IIR and FIR digital filters based on: (i) phase response, (ii) stability, (iii) computational complexity, and (iv) type of filter realizations.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IIR Filter | FIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Phase response** | Generally has **non-linear phase**; can be made linear-phase only for special allpass equalizer stages. | Can have **exactly linear phase** when coefficients are symmetric. |
| **(ii) Stability** | Conditionally stable — all poles must lie **strictly inside the unit circle**; quantization can destabilize. | **Always stable** since poles are at $z = 0$ (inside the unit circle). |
| **(iii) Computational complexity** | **Lower order** $N$ for same magnitude specs (sharp transition achieved with poles). | **Higher order** $N$ required (no poles to sharpen roll-off). |
| **(iv) Realizations** | Direct Form-I, Direct Form-II, Cascade, Parallel — all involve **feedback**. | Direct Form, Cascade, Linear-Phase, Frequency Sampling — **no feedback**. |

> *Plus: IIR is suited to applications requiring sharp magnitude selectivity; FIR is preferred where linear phase is critical (e.g., data transmission, audio mastering).*

**(b)** *A digital IIR lowpass filter must satisfy: $0.95 \le \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 1$ for $0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le 0.3\pi$, and $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le 0.02$ for $0.5\pi \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$. Express these in dB and identify the four specification parameters.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Read the linear specs:**

From the passband: lower bound $= 1 - \delta_p = 0.95$, so $\delta_p = 0.05$.
From the stopband: $\delta_s = 0.02$.

**Step 2 — Convert passband to dB:**

$$
A_p = -20\log_{10}(1 - \delta_p) = -20\log_{10}(0.95) = -20 \times (-0.02228) = 0.4455 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert stopband to dB:**

$$
A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s) = -20\log_{10}(0.02) = -20 \times (-1.69897) = 33.98 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — State the four specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Passband edge $\omega_p$ | $0.3\pi$ rad/sample |
| Stopband edge $\omega_s$ | $0.5\pi$ rad/sample |
| Passband ripple $A_p$ | $\approx 0.45$ dB |
| Stopband attenuation $A_s$ | $\approx 34$ dB |

**Step 5 — Verification of original constraints:** Substituting back: $10^{-A_p/20} = 10^{-0.4455/20} = 10^{-0.02228} = 0.95$ ✓ and $10^{-A_s/20} = 10^{-33.98/20} = 0.02$ ✓.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to pre-warp:** When using Bilinear Transformation, students directly substitute $\omega$ for $\Omega$ without pre-warping. This causes the digital filter to fail the stopband spec near $\omega \approx \pi$. **Always pre-warp $\omega_p$ and $\omega_s$ to $\Omega_p$ and $\Omega_s$ using $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$.**
>
> 2. **Using $\delta_p$ where dB is required:** Converting $A_p$ to $\delta_p$ is mandatory when the analog prototype equation uses linear terms (e.g., Butterworth: $1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N} \le 10^{0.1 A_p}$). Confusing the two costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Not rounding up the order $N$:** $N$ must always be an **integer** $\ge$ the calculated value. If the formula gives $N = 5.46$, the answer is $\mathbf{6}$ — not 5.5 and not 5.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $T$ in BLT:** The standard KTU convention often sets $T = 1$ or $T = 2$ — read the problem carefully. Mixing $T$ values across pre-warping and BLT substitution loses full marks in part (b).
>
> 5. **Confusing $A_p$ with $A_s$ in dB signs:** $A_p$ is small (e.g., 1 dB) and $A_s$ is large (e.g., 40 dB). The sign convention is **positive** for both ripple *amounts*; the underlying magnitude is *negative* in dB.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **IIR filter definition:** Recursive filter with rational $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ and infinite-duration impulse response.
> - **Four mandatory specs:** $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$, $A_p$, $A_s$ (or equivalently $\delta_p$, $\delta_s$).
> - **Tolerance scheme:** Passband magnitude $\in [1-\delta_p, 1]$; stopband magnitude $\le \delta_s$; transition band free.
> - **dB conversion:** $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p)$, $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$.
> - **Inverse conversion:** $\delta_p = 1 - 10^{-A_p/20}$, $\delta_s = 10^{-A_s/20}$.
> - **Bilinear pre-warping:** $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$ — mandatory before analog design.
> - **Butterworth order formula:** $N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{0.1 A_s}-1}{10^{0.1 A_p}-1}\right)}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$.
> - **Stability requirement:** All poles of $H(z)$ must satisfy $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ (inside the unit circle).
> - **IIR vs FIR:** IIR is magnitude-efficient (low order); FIR is linear-phase and always stable.
> - **KTU must-know analog prototypes:** Butterworth (maximally flat passband), Chebyshev-I (equiripple passband), Chebyshev-II (equiripple stopband), Elliptic (equiripple both).
> - **Final digital $H(z)$:** Obtained by substituting $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ in $H_a(s)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of IIR Digital Filters from Analog Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of IIR Digital Filters from Analog Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant (LSI) system whose impulse response extends over an infinite number of samples, and whose transfer function is a *rational function* of $z^{-1}$ containing both feed-forward and feed-back terms.

Mathematically, the IIR digital filter transfer function is expressed as:

$$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}, \quad a_k \neq 0 \text{ for at least one } k \geq 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** The design of IIR digital filters is performed by first designing a well-established **analog prototype filter** (Butterworth, Chebyshev, or Elliptic) in the continuous-time *s*-domain, and then applying a **sampling/transformation technique** that maps the analog *s*-plane to the digital *z*-plane. The two canonical mapping methods studied in Module 3 are:
> 1. **Impulse Invariance Method**
> 2. **Bilinear Transformation Method**

## 1.2 The "Why" — Why Design Digital IIR Filters From Analog?

The IIR design problem in the digital domain is mathematically harder than the analog design problem. Over decades, analog filter theory (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, Elliptic) has been rigorously developed with closed-form equations, well-tabulated coefficients, and proven design procedures. Engineers therefore prefer to:

1. Design a proven analog filter $H_a(s)$.
2. Apply a mathematical transformation $s \to z$ to obtain $H(z)$.
3. Implement the resulting $H(z)$ digitally.

This **indirect approach** exploits the maturity of analog filter theory to obtain a *guaranteed stable* digital IIR filter, provided the mapping preserves stability.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of designing a digital IIR filter as **renovating a heritage building**.
> - The **analog filter** is the *original architect's blueprint* — it has been refined over 100+ years of engineering practice and is trusted, documented, and easy to compute.
> - The **digital filter** is the *modern, earthquake-resistant, software-rendered 3D model* built for the digital world.
> - The **mapping method** (Impulse Invariance or Bilinear) is the *conversion rule* that translates every wall, column, and beam from the old blueprint into a structurally sound digital equivalent.
>
> Just as a poor blueprint-to-3D conversion can collapse a building, a poor *s*-to-*z* mapping can make the digital filter unstable or distort its frequency response. The two mapping methods (Impulse Invariance, Bilinear Transformation) are the two engineering "rule-books" that guarantee a sound digital structure.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics in IIR Design

- **$\Omega_p$** — Analog passband edge frequency in **rad/sec**.
- **$\Omega_s$** — Analog stopband edge frequency in **rad/sec**.
- **$\omega_p$** — Digital passband edge frequency in **rad/sample**.
- **$\omega_s$** — Digital stopband edge frequency in **rad/sample**.
- **$\delta_p$** — Passband ripple (tolerance).
- **$\delta_s$** — Stopband attenuation (tolerance).
- **$A_p = -20\log_{10}(1-\delta_p)$ dB** — Passband ripple in **decibels**.
- **$A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ dB** — Stopband attenuation in **decibels**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Core Steps of IIR Design

The IIR digital filter design procedure is universally reduced to **three sequential stages**:

1. **Specification Translation:** Convert the digital frequency specifications $(\omega_p, \omega_s, A_p, A_s)$ to analog frequency specifications $(\Omega_p, \Omega_s, A_p, A_s)$ using either *direct* ($\Omega = \omega/T$) or *pre-warped* ($\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$) relations.
2. **Analog Prototype Design:** Compute the analog transfer function $H_a(s)$ of order $N$ that satisfies the analog specs.
3. **Mapping to Digital Domain:** Apply either Impulse Invariance or Bilinear Transformation to obtain $H(z)$.

## 2.2 Analog Filter Approximations (The Prototypes)

### 2.2.1 Butterworth Filter
Maximally flat magnitude response in the passband. The **squared magnitude** is:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

- $N$ = filter order, $\Omega_c$ = cutoff frequency ($-3$ dB point).
- Monotonically decreasing; no ripples in either band.
- All poles lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the left-half *s*-plane, spaced by $\pi/N$ radians.

### 2.2.2 Chebyshev Type-I Filter
Equiripple in the passband, monotonic in the stopband.

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 \, T_N^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

where $T_N(x)$ is the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind of order $N$, and $\epsilon$ controls passband ripple.

### 2.2.3 Chebyshev Type-II (Inverse) Filter
Monotonic in the passband, equiripple in the stopband. Provides a *flatter passband* than Type-I at the cost of slower roll-off.

### 2.2.4 Elliptic (Cauer) Filter
Equiripple in *both* the passband and the stopband. Achieves the **sharpest transition** for a given order $N$, but has the most complex phase response.

## 2.3 The Two Mapping Methods — A Priori Comparison

| Property | Impulse Invariance | Bilinear Transformation |
|:---------|:-------------------|:------------------------|
| Mapping equation | $h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ | $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ |
| Mapping type | One-to-one on imaginary axis (with aliasing) | One-to-one entire *j*Ω axis to unit circle |
| Frequency relation | $\omega = \Omega T$ (linear) | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ (non-linear) |
| Aliasing | **Yes** (major drawback) | **None** (eliminates aliasing completely) |
| Stability preservation | Yes (LHP maps to inside unit circle) | Yes (LHP maps to inside unit circle) |
| Suitable filter types | Strictly band-limited (e.g., LPF, BPF) | **All** filter types including HPF, BSF |
| Best for | LPF and band-limited BPF simulations | General-purpose digital filter design |
| Order preservation | $N$ is preserved | $N$ is preserved |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Usage |
|:--|:--------|:-------------------|
| 1 | $H(z) = T \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{A_k}{1 - e^{p_k T}z^{-1}}$ | Impulse Invariance — partial fraction form |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | Bilinear transformation: analog → digital |
| 3 | $z = \dfrac{1 + (T/2)s}{1 - (T/2)s}$ | Bilinear transformation: digital → analog (inverse) |
| 4 | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Frequency warping relation |
| 5 | $\Omega_p^{\text{pre}} = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_p}{2}\right)$ | Pre-warping at passband edge |
| 6 | $\Omega_s^{\text{pre}} = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_s}{2}\right)$ | Pre-warping at stopband edge |
| 7 | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{T}\displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\!\left(j\dfrac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)$ | Aliasing relation in Impulse Invariance |
| 8 | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right]}{2\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$ | Minimum Butterworth order |
| 9 | $p_k = \Omega_c \, e^{j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)}, \; k = 1,2,\dots,N$ | Butterworth pole locations (LHP) |
| 10 | $\epsilon = \sqrt{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}$ | Passband ripple parameter (Chebyshev-I) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize formulas 2, 3, 4, and 5 — these are the four formulas that appear in **almost every Module-3 question** on Bilinear Transformation. Formula 1 (Impulse Invariance partial-fraction) is mandatory for any Impulse Invariance problem.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

IIR digital filters designed from analog prototypes are deployed in:

- **Audio processing software** (equalizers, noise-cancelling headphones).
- **Biomedical signal processing** (ECG/EEG filtering) where sharp transition bands are required with low filter order.
- **Communication receivers** (matched filtering, channel selection) where high stopband attenuation is critical.
- **Control systems** (digital controllers emulating analog PID).
- **Seismic data acquisition** systems where analog pre-whitening filters are emulated in software.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Impulse Invariance Method

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise
Let $H_a(s)$ be a causal, stable analog filter. Its impulse response is $h_a(t)$, obtained by the inverse Laplace transform:

$$h_a(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H_a(s)\}$$

### 3.1.2 Sampling the Impulse Response
The Impulse Invariance method *samples* the analog impulse response at uniform intervals $t = nT$ to obtain a discrete-time sequence:

$$h(n) = h_a(t)\Big\vert_{t = nT} = h_a(nT)$$

To ensure correct gain matching, the sampled sequence is scaled by $T$:

$$h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$$

> **Step Logic:** Scaling by $T$ is required so that $\sum_{n} h(n) \approx \int h_a(t)\,dt$, preserving the DC gain of the analog filter when converted to digital.

### 3.1.3 Partial Fraction Expansion of $H_a(s)$
Express $H_a(s)$ as a sum of simple first-order terms (assuming distinct poles):

$$H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{A_k}{s - p_k}$$

The inverse Laplace transform gives:

$$h_a(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k e^{p_k t} u(t)$$

Sampling at $t = nT$:

$$h(n) = T \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k e^{p_k nT} u(n)$$

### 3.1.4 Taking the Z-Transform
Apply $\mathcal{Z}\{\cdot\}$ to the sampled sequence. Recall $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u(n)\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \mathcal{Z}\{h(n)\} \\
     &= T \sum_{k=1}^{N} A_k \mathcal{Z}\{e^{p_k nT} u(n)\} \\
     &= T \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{A_k}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each analog pole $p_k$ at location $s = p_k$ in the *s*-plane is mapped to a digital pole at $z = e^{p_k T}$ in the *z*-plane. The factor $e^{p_k T}$ is the **exponential mapping**.

### 3.1.5 The Aliasing Relation
Taking the DTFT of $h(n) = T h_a(nT)$ gives the aliasing identity:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} H_a\!\left(j \frac{\omega - 2\pi k}{T}\right)$$

> **Why this matters:** If $H_a(j\Omega)$ is **not strictly band-limited to** $\vert \Omega \vert < \pi/T$, the shifted copies overlap and **aliasing** distorts the digital frequency response. This is the central weakness of Impulse Invariance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bilinear Transformation

### 3.2.1 Motivation — Numerical Integration Viewpoint
A causal analog filter $H_a(s)$ can be described by a linear constant-coefficient differential equation. The Bilinear Transformation is derived by replacing the analog **differentiator** $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ with the numerical **trapezoidal-rule integrator**.

Recall that an integrator has transfer function $\dfrac{1}{s}$. The trapezoidal rule gives:

$$y(n) = y(n-1) + \frac{T}{2}\bigl[x(n) + x(n-1)\bigr]$$

### 3.2.2 Transfer Function of the Trapezoidal Integrator
Take the *z*-transform of the difference equation:

$$Y(z) = z^{-1}Y(z) + \frac{T}{2}\bigl[X(z) + z^{-1}X(z)\bigr]$$

Solving for $Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$Y(z)\left[1 - z^{-1}\right] = \frac{T}{2}X(z)\left[1 + z^{-1}\right]$$

$$\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{T}{2}\cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

> **Step Logic:** This is the digital integrator's transfer function. Since the analog integrator's transfer function is $1/s$, we can equate the two to find the substitution rule.

### 3.2.3 Equating Analog and Digital Integrators
Set $\dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{T}{2}\cdot\dfrac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$ and solve for $s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{s} &= \frac{T}{2}\cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} \\
s &= \frac{2}{T}\cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} \\
z &= \frac{1 + (T/2)s}{1 - (T/2)s}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **Bilinear Transformation**. To convert an analog transfer function $H_a(s)$ to digital, substitute $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ everywhere in $H_a(s)$.

### 3.2.4 Stability Preservation
Substitute $s = \sigma + j\Omega$ into $z = \dfrac{1 + (T/2)s}{1 - (T/2)s}$:

$$z = \frac{(1 + T\sigma/2) + j(T\Omega/2)}{(1 - T\sigma/2) - j(T\Omega/2)}$$

The magnitude is:

$$|z|^2 = \frac{(1 + T\sigma/2)^2 + (T\Omega/2)^2}{(1 - T\sigma/2)^2 + (T\Omega/2)^2}$$

- If $\sigma < 0$ (LHP), then $(1 + T\sigma/2) < (1 - T\sigma/2)$, giving $|z| < 1$ → maps **inside** the unit circle (stable).
- If $\sigma = 0$ (imaginary axis), $|z| = 1$ → maps **onto** the unit circle.
- If $\sigma > 0$ (RHP), $|z| > 1$ → maps **outside** the unit circle (unstable).

> **Conclusion:** The bilinear transformation is a **conformal map** that preserves stability.

### 3.2.5 Derivation of Frequency Warping
Set $s = j\Omega$ in the transformation $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ and evaluate at $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}}{1 + e^{-j\omega}}$$

Apply the half-angle identities $1 - e^{-j\omega} = 2j\sin(\omega/2)e^{-j\omega/2}$ and $1 + e^{-j\omega} = 2\cos(\omega/2)e^{-j\omega/2}$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\cdot \frac{2j\sin(\omega/2)e^{-j\omega/2}}{2\cos(\omega/2)e^{-j\omega/2}} = \frac{2j}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$

> **Interpretation:** The analog frequency $\Omega$ is *non-linearly compressed* into the digital frequency $\omega$. Low frequencies are mapped nearly linearly, but high frequencies are severely compressed — this is the **frequency warping** effect.

### 3.2.6 Pre-Warping Procedure
To compensate for warping, the **critical analog frequency** (typically the passband edge $\Omega_p$) is **pre-warped** *before* designing the analog prototype:

$$\Omega_p' = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right), \qquad \Omega_s' = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right)$$

The analog prototype is then designed so that it meets specifications **at the pre-warped critical frequencies**. After the bilinear transformation is applied, the pre-warped $\omega_p$ maps back exactly to the desired $\omega_p$, ensuring specifications are met at the edge(s) of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Step:** In any Bilinear Transformation design problem with non-linear warping, you must apply pre-warping to **all critical frequencies** before designing the analog prototype. Failure to do this is the #1 reason students lose marks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Design a single-pole Butterworth low-pass digital filter with 3 dB cutoff at $\omega_c = \pi/4$ rad/sample, using the Bilinear Transformation with $T = 1$ sec. The analog prototype is $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s + 1}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the cutoff frequency:**

$$\Omega_c' = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right) = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi/4}{2}\right) = 2\tan\!\left(\frac{\pi}{8}\right) = 2(0.4142) = 0.8284 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency-scale the analog prototype to use the pre-warped cutoff:**

The analog prototype has cutoff at $\Omega = 1$. Scale: $s \to s/\Omega_c' = s/0.8284$.

$$H_a'(s) = \frac{0.8284}{s + 0.8284}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Bilinear Transformation:**

Substitute $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = 2\cdot\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.8284}{2\cdot\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} + 0.8284}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + z^{-1})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H(z) &= \frac{0.8284(1 + z^{-1})}{2(1 - z^{-1}) + 0.8284(1 + z^{-1})} \\
     &= \frac{0.8284 + 0.8284 z^{-1}}{(2 + 0.8284) + (-2 + 0.8284) z^{-1}} \\
     &= \frac{0.8284 + 0.8284 z^{-1}}{2.8284 - 1.1716 z^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the coefficients** (divide by $2.8284$):

$$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{0.2929 + 0.2929 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.4142 z^{-1}}}$$

> **Verification:** At $\omega = 0$, $H(1) = \dfrac{0.2929 + 0.2929}{1 - 0.4142} = \dfrac{0.5858}{0.5858} = 1$ (DC gain preserved). At $\omega = \pi/4$, $|H(e^{j\pi/4})| = 1/\sqrt{2}$ ($-3$ dB cutoff satisfied). ✓

The following diagram illustrates the two mapping methods and their effect on the analog-to-digital conversion of the imaginary axis (frequency response).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answers Provided.** These are calibrated to fetch **full 3 marks** in KTU valuation.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** State the two methods used to design IIR digital filters from analog filters. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two standard methods are:

1. **Impulse Invariance Method:** In this method, the digital filter's impulse response $h(n)$ is obtained by *sampling the impulse response* of the analog prototype: $h(n) = T \cdot h_a(nT)$.
   - **Advantage:** The digital filter preserves the *time-domain characteristics* (impulse response shape) of the analog prototype exactly.
   - **Disadvantage:** Suffers from *aliasing* of the frequency response, restricting its use to strictly band-limited analog filters.
