Stopband Attenuation $A_s$ (dB) | |---|---|---| | Rectangular | $0.89\cdot 2\pi/N$ | **21** | | Hanning | $3.11\cdot 2\pi/N$ | **44** | | Hamming | $3.32\cdot 2\pi/N$ | **53** | | Blackman | $5.56\cdot 2\pi/N$ | **74** | | Kaiser ($\beta = 4.86$) | $2.93\cdot 2\pi/N$ | **50** | | Kaiser ($\beta = 8.96$) | $4.32\cdot 2\pi/N$ | **90** | ## 3.5 Worked Example — Hamming Window LPF Design **Problem:** Design an FIR lowpass filter with $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample, $N = 9$ taps, using Hamming window. **Step 1 — Group delay.** $$\alpha = (N-1)/2 = (9-1)/2 = 4 \text{ samples}$$ **Step 2 — Ideal impulse response.** $$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(0.4\pi(n-4))}{\pi(n-4)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 8$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate at each index.** $$\begin{aligned} h_d[0] &= \frac{\sin(0.4\pi(-4))}{\pi(-4)} = \frac{\sin(-1.6\pi)}{-4\pi} = \frac{-0.9511}{-12.566} = 0.0757 \\ h_d[1] &= \frac{\sin(0.4\pi(-3))}{\pi(-3)} = \frac{\sin(-1.2\pi)}{-3\pi} = \frac{-0.8090}{-9.425} = 0.0858 \\ h_d[2] &= \frac{\sin(0.4\pi(-2))}{\pi(-2)} = \frac{\sin(-0.8\pi)}{-2\pi} = \frac{-0.5878}{-6.283} = 0.0935 \\ h_d[3] &= \frac{\sin(0.4\pi(-1))}{\pi(-1)} = \frac{\sin(-0.4\pi)}{-\pi} = \frac{-0.9511}{-3.142} = 0.3027 \\ h_d[4] &= \frac{\sin(0)}{\pi \cdot 0} = 0.4 \text{ (limit)} \\ h_d[5] &= h_d[3] = 0.3027 \quad \text{(symmetry)} \\ h_d[6] &= h_d[2] = 0.0935 \\ h_d[7] &= h_d[1] = 0.0858 \\ h_d[8] &= h_d[0] = 0.0757 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Hamming window values.** $$w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{8}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 8$$ $$\begin{aligned} w[0] &= 0.54 - 0.46\cos(0) = 0.08 \\ w[1] &= 0.54 - 0.46\cos(\pi/4) = 0.54 - 0.46(0.707) = 0.215 \\ w[2] &= 0.54 - 0.46\cos(\pi/2) = 0.54 \\ w[3] &= 0.54 - 0.46\cos(3\pi/4) = 0.865 \\ w[4] &= 0.54 - 0.46\cos(\pi) = 1.00 \\ w[5] &= w[3] = 0.865 \\ w[6] &= w[2] = 0.54 \\ w[7] &= w[1] = 0.215 \\ w[8] &= w[0] = 0.08 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Multiply to obtain $h[n] = h_d[n]\cdot w[n]$.** $$\begin{aligned} h[0] &= 0.0757 \times 0.08 = 0.0061 \\ h[1] &= 0.0858 \times 0.215 = 0.0184 \\ h[2] &= 0.0935 \times 0.54 = 0.0505 \\ h[3] &= 0.3027 \times 0.865 = 0.2618 \\ h[4] &= 0.4 \times 1.00 = 0.4000 \\ h[5] &= 0.3027 \times 0.865 = 0.2618 \\ h[6] &= 0.0935 \times 0.54 = 0.0505 \\ h[7] &= 0.0858 \times 0.215 = 0.0184 \\ h[8] &= 0.0757 \times 0.08 = 0.0061 \end{aligned}$$ **Verification:** $\sum_{n=0}^{8} h[n] \approx 1.0736$ (close to expected gain = $\omega_c/\pi \cdot \sum w[n]$). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Direct Form FIR Filter Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIn["Input x[n]"] --> nodeD0["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD0 --> nodeD1["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD1 --> nodeD2["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD2 --> nodeD3["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD3 --> nodeD4["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD4 --> nodeD5["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD5 --> nodeD6["z^-1 Delay"] nodeD6 --> nodeD7["z^-1 Delay"] nodeIn --> nodeM0["x h0"] nodeD0 --> nodeM1["x h1"] nodeD1 --> nodeM2["x h2"] nodeD2 --> nodeM3["x h3"] nodeD3 --> nodeM4["x h4"] nodeD4 --> nodeM5["x h5"] nodeD5 --> nodeM6["x h6"] nodeD6 --> nodeM7["x h7"] nodeD7 --> nodeM8["x h8"] nodeM0 --> nodeSum["Sum Σ"] nodeM1 --> nodeSum nodeM2 --> nodeSum nodeM3 --> nodeSum nodeM4 --> nodeSum nodeM5 --> nodeSum nodeM6 --> nodeSum nodeM7 --> nodeSum nodeM8 --> nodeSum nodeSum --> nodeOut["Output y[n]"] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — FIR Filter Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Step A: Define Specifications
ωp, ωs, δp, δs"] --> nodeIdeal["Step B: Specify Ideal Hd ejω
LPF HPF BPF BSF"] nodeIdeal --> nodeInv["Step C: Compute hd n via
Inverse DTFT"] nodeInv --> nodeChoice{"Step D: Choose
Design Method?"} nodeChoice -- Window Method --> nodeWin["Select Window
Rect Hanning
Hamming Blackman Kaiser"] nodeChoice -- Frequency Sampling --> nodeFreq["Sample Hd at N points
Apply IDFT"] nodeWin --> nodeTrunc["Multiply hd n with w n
Truncate to N samples"] nodeFreq --> nodeTrunc nodeTrunc --> nodeVerify["Step E: Verify
Specifications"] nodeVerify --> nodeDone["Step F: Final Coefficients h 0 to h N-1"] style nodeStart fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style nodeIdeal fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style nodeDone fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style nodeChoice fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting Linear Phase Filter Type ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeRoot{"Choose Filter Type"} nodeRoot -- LPF --> nodeLPF{"Symmetric or
Anti-symmetric?"} nodeRoot -- HPF --> nodeHPF{"Symmetric or
Anti-symmetric?"} nodeRoot -- BPF --> nodeBPF{"Symmetric or
Anti-symmetric?"} nodeRoot -- BSF --> nodeBSF{"Symmetric or
Anti-symmetric?"} nodeLPF -- Symmetric N odd --> nodeT1["Type I LPF OK"] nodeLPF -- Symmetric N even --> nodeT2["Type II LPF OK"] nodeLPF -- Anti-sym N odd --> nodeT3["Type III NOT POSSIBLE
Zero at ω=0"] nodeLPF -- Anti-sym N even --> nodeT4["Type IV NOT POSSIBLE
Zero at ω=0"] nodeHPF -- Symmetric N odd --> nodeT5["Type I HPF OK"] nodeHPF -- Symmetric N even --> nodeT6["Type II NOT POSSIBLE
Zero at ω=π"] nodeHPF -- Anti-sym N odd --> nodeT7["Type III NOT POSSIBLE
Zero at ω=0"] nodeHPF -- Anti-sym N even --> nodeT8["Type IV HPF OK"] nodeBSF -- Symmetric N odd --> nodeT9["Type I BSF OK"] nodeBSF -- Symmetric N even --> nodeT10["Type II NOT POSSIBLE"] nodeBSF -- Anti-sym N odd --> nodeT11["Type III BSF OK"] nodeBSF -- Anti-sym N even --> nodeT12["Type IV BSF OK"] style nodeRoot fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style nodeT1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style nodeT2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style nodeT3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style nodeT4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style nodeT5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style nodeT6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style nodeT7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 nodeT8["Type IV HPF OK"]:::ok classDef ok fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Truncation and Gibbs Phenomenon ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Ideal hd n
Infinite Duration
Sinc Function"] --> nodeMul["Multiplication in
Time Domain"] nodeWin["Window w n
Finite Duration"] --> nodeMul nodeMul --> nodeOut["Truncated h n
Finite Length N"] nodeMul -.-> nodeConv["Convolution in
Frequency Domain"] nodeFreqA["Ideal Hd ejω
Sharp Rectangular"] --> nodeConv nodeFreqW["Window W ejω
Main Lobe + Side Lobes"] --> nodeConv nodeConv --> nodeFreqOut["Actual H ejω
Rippled Response
Gibbs Phenomenon"] style nodeA fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style nodeWin fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style nodeOut fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style nodeFreqOut fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State and explain the conditions for an FIR filter to have linear phase response.** **Model Answer:** An FIR filter has linear phase response if its impulse response satisfies one of two symmetry conditions: 1. **Even symmetry:** $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$ (symmetric) 2. **Odd symmetry:** $h[n] = -h[N-1-n]$ (anti-symmetric) This ensures that the phase response $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha\omega \pm \beta$ is a linear function of $\omega$, where the constant group delay $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ causes all frequency components to be delayed equally, preserving the signal waveform. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Compare the Window Method and Frequency Sampling Method of FIR filter design.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Window Method | Frequency Sampling Method | |---|---|---| | Basis | Time-domain truncation | Frequency-domain sampling | | Flexibility | Limited to standard windows | Arbitrary magnitude samples allowed | | Control | Indirect (via window choice) | Direct (control at each frequency) | | Complexity | Simple (closed-form $h_d[n]$) | Moderate (requires IDFT) | | Best For | Standard LPF/HPF design | Filters with arbitrary response shape | --- ## **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Derive the condition for linear phase in an FIR filter. Explain the four types of linear phase FIR filters.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Start with frequency response of $N$-tap FIR filter.** $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$ **Step 2 — Apply symmetry substitution $n \to N-1-n$.** $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[N-1-n]\, e^{-j\omega(N-1-n)}$$ **Step 3 — Average both forms and impose $h[n] = \pm h[N-1-n]$.** $$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}\left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a[n]\cos(\omega n) \mp \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} b[n]\sin(\omega n)\right]$$ The factor $e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$ provides linear phase with group delay $\alpha = (N-1)/2$. **Step 4 — Tabulate the four types.** | Type | Symmetry | Length $N$ | $H(e^{j0})$ | $H(e^{j\pi})$ | Suitable For | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | I | Symmetric | Odd | Non-zero | Non-zero | All filters | | II | Symmetric | Even | Non-zero | Zero | LPF, BPF only | | III | Anti-symmetric | Odd | Zero | Zero | BPF, BSF only | | IV | Anti-symmetric | Even | Zero | Non-zero | HPF, BSF only | --- #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Design an FIR lowpass filter using the Hanning window with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/3$ rad/sample and $N = 7$.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute group delay and ideal impulse response.** $$\alpha = (7-1)/2 = 3, \quad h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\pi/3 \cdot (n-3))}{\pi(n-3)}$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate $h_d[n]$ at $n = 0, 1, \ldots, 6$.** $$\begin{aligned} h_d[0] &= \frac{\sin(-\pi)}{-3\pi} = 0 \\ h_d[1] &= \frac{\sin(-2\pi/3)}{-2\pi} = \frac{0.866}{6.283} = 0.1378 \\ h_d[2] &= \frac{\sin(-\pi/3)}{-\pi} = \frac{0.866}{3.142} = 0.2757 \\ h_d[3] &= \frac{\sin(0)}{0} = 1/3 = 0.3333 \\ h_d[4] &= h_d[2] = 0.2757 \\ h_d[5] &= h_d[1] = 0.1378 \\ h_d[6] &= h_d[0] = 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Hanning window.** $$w[n] = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{6}\right)$$ $$\begin{aligned} w[0] &= 0.5 - 0.5\cos(0) = 0 \\ w[1] &= 0.5 - 0.5\cos(\pi/3) = 0.25 \\ w[2] &= 0.5 - 0.5\cos(2\pi/3) = 0.75 \\ w[3] &= 0.5 - 0.5\cos(\pi) = 1.00 \\ w[4] &= w[2] = 0.75 \\ w[5] &= w[1] = 0.25 \\ w[6] &= w[0] = 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply to obtain $h[n]$.** $$\begin{aligned} h[0] &= 0 \times 0 = 0 \\ h[1] &= 0.1378 \times 0.25 = 0.0345 \\ h[2] &= 0.2757 \times 0.75 = 0.2068 \\ h[3] &= 0.3333 \times 1.00 = 0.3333 \\ h[4] &= 0.2757 \times 0.75 = 0.2068 \\ h[5] &= 0.1378 \times 0.25 = 0.0345 \\ h[6] &= 0 \times 0 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Result:** $h[n] = \{0, 0.0345, 0.2068, 0.3333, 0.2068, 0.0345, 0\}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Explain the frequency sampling method for FIR filter design. Mention its advantages over the window method.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Concept.** The frequency sampling method designs FIR filters by specifying the desired frequency response at a finite set of equally spaced frequencies and using the IDFT to obtain the filter coefficients. **Step 2 — Sampling frequencies.** $$\omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ $$H[k] = H_d(e^{j\omega_k})$$ **Step 3 — IDFT computation.** $$h[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} H[k]\, e^{j2\pi kn/N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$ **Step 4 — Real-coefficient constraint.** $$H[N-k] = H^*[k]$$ **Step 5 — Transition band optimization (Donovan's method).** Introduce unconstrained samples $T_1, T_2$ at frequencies between passband and stopband to reduce Gibbs oscillations and improve stopband attenuation. **Advantages over window method:** 1. Direct control over frequency response at sampled points. 2. Useful for filters with non-standard magnitude shapes. 3. Efficient when filter length is large (uses FFT). --- #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Compare the Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, and Blackman windows in terms of main lobe width and minimum stopband attenuation.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** | Window | Main Lobe Width (in $2\pi/N$) | Min. Stopband Attenuation (dB) | Transition Width | |---|---|---|---| | Rectangular | **0.89** | **21** | $0.89 \cdot 2\pi/N$ | | Hanning | **3.11** | **44** | $3.11 \cdot 2\pi/N$ | | Hamming | **3.32** | **53** | $3.32 \cdot 2\pi/N$ | | Blackman | **5.56** | **74** | $5.56 \cdot 2\pi/N$ | **Key observations:** - **Rectangular** has the narrowest main lobe (best resolution) but the **highest side lobes** (worst stopband attenuation). - **Blackman** has the widest main lobe (worst resolution) but the **best stopband attenuation** (74 dB). - **Trade-off rule:** Wider main lobe ↔ Better stopband attenuation. - For a given transition width, filter order must increase as window gets wider. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting symmetry** — When evaluating $h_d[n]$ for filter design problems, students often compute only the first half and forget $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$. Always exploit symmetry to halve computation. > 2. **Sign errors in anti-symmetric case** — Anti-symmetric means $h[n] = -h[N-1-n]$, not $+$. Verify by checking that $h[\alpha] = 0$ at the center. > 3. **Window endpoint formula** — Hanning/Hamming formula uses $\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$, not $2\pi n/N$. Off-by-one errors cost 1–2 marks. > 4. **Filter type selection** — Choosing Type II for HPF or Type III for LPF violates the zero-at-$\omega$ constraint. Always check the **suitable for** column in the table. > 5. **Gibbs phenomenon** — Windowing cannot eliminate ripples entirely; it only reduces their amplitude. Don't claim "zero ripple" in answers. > 6. **Unit confusion** — $\omega_c$ in **rad/sample** vs **Hz** vs **normalized frequency** ($f_c/0.5$). KTU problems typically use rad/sample. --- ## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** ### **Rapid Revision Checklist** - [x] **FIR** = Finite Impulse Response = **no feedback** = always stable. - [x] **Linear phase condition:** Impulse response must be **symmetric** or **anti-symmetric** about center index $(N-1)/2$. - [x] **Group delay** $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ for all linear phase FIR filters. - [x] **Four types** — I (sym, odd $N$), II (sym, even $N$), III (anti-sym, odd $N$), IV (anti-sym, even $N$). - [x] **Type II** cannot realize HPF/BSF due to $H(e^{j\pi}) = 0$. - [x] **Type III** cannot realize LPF/HPF due to $H(e^{j0}) = H(e^{j\pi}) = 0$. - [x] **Type IV** cannot realize LPF/BPF due to $H(e^{j0}) = 0$. - [x] **Window method:** $h[n] = h_d[n]\cdot w[n]$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$. - [x] **Window trade-off:** Wider main lobe ↔ Lower side lobes ↔ Better stopband attenuation. - [x] **Frequency sampling:** $h[n] = \text{IDFT}\{H[k]\}$ with $H[N-k] = H^*[k]$ for real filters. - [x] **Hanning window:** $w[n] = 0.5 - 0.5\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$. - [x] **Hamming window:** $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$. - [x] **Blackman window:** $w[n] = 0.42 - 0.5\cos(2\pi n/(N-1)) + 0.08\cos(4\pi n/(N-1))$. - [x] **Ideal LPF impulse response:** $h_d[n] = \sin(\omega_c(n-\alpha))/[\pi(n-\alpha)]$. - [x] **Gibbs phenomenon** causes oscillations near discontinuities in ideal responses — windowing reduces but does not eliminate them. - [x] **Advantage of FIR over IIR:** Guaranteed stability + exact linear phase + no limit cycles. - [x] **Disadvantage:** Higher filter order required (typically $5\times$ to $10\times$ more taps than equivalent IIR).

topics.title: Impulse Response of Ideal Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Impulse Response of Ideal Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Discrete-Time Signal Processing (DTSP), an **ideal filter** is a theoretical, mathematically perfect frequency-selective system whose frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ has a constant magnitude (either unity or zero) with abrupt, discontinuous transitions (brick-wall characteristics) and a purely linear phase response over the passband. The **impulse response $h(n)$** of an ideal filter is obtained by computing the inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of its ideal frequency response.

The four classical ideal filters studied under Module 4 are:

| Filter Type | Function |
|---|---|
| **Ideal Low Pass Filter (ILPF)** | Passes low frequencies, blocks high frequencies |
| **Ideal High Pass Filter (IHPF)** | Passes high frequencies, blocks low frequencies |
| **Ideal Band Pass Filter (IBPF)** | Passes a band of frequencies |
| **Ideal Band Stop Filter (IBSF)** | Stops a band of frequencies (notch filter) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 4 mandates derivation of the impulse response of **all four ideal filters** using the inverse DTFT integral. Students must memorize the boundary conditions for $\omega$ in each filter type, as marks are routinely awarded for correctly writing the limits of integration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **garden hose with a perfect filter attachment**. An Ideal Low Pass Filter is like a sieve that lets only *large rocks* (low-frequency, slowly-varying components like the DC offset or the base note of a song) pass through, while completely stopping *sand grains* (high-frequency noise like hiss). 

The **impulse response** is the "fingerprint" of this sieve. If you tap the sieve once with a single hammer blow (an impulse $\delta(n)$), the sieve responds by vibrating with a very specific, mathematically perfect pattern — a **sinc function**. This sinc-shaped vibration tells you everything about how the sieve will respond to *any* subsequent input signal (thanks to LTI convolution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** Ideal filters are **non-causal** (the impulse response exists for negative time, $n < 0$) and have **infinite length**. Therefore, they cannot be built in real-time hardware. They serve as theoretical benchmarks against which practical (realizable) FIR and IIR filters are compared.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Convention)

- **Cutoff Frequency** ($\omega_c$) — measured in **radians/sample**, range $[0, \pi]$.
- **Sampling Convention** — Discrete-time frequencies are normalized: $\omega = \Omega T$ where $\Omega$ is analog frequency and $T$ is sampling period.
- **DC Gain** — value of $H(e^{j0}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n)$.
- **Phase Linearity** — phase $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha \omega$ for a constant group delay of $\alpha$ samples.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Ideal Filters Exist

An LTI discrete-time system is fully characterized by its frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ or equivalently its impulse response $h(n)$, linked by the **DTFT pair**:

$$
H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

$$
h(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega
$$

For an ideal filter, we *specify* the desired $H(e^{j\omega})$ as a piecewise constant rectangular window in the frequency domain, then compute $h(n)$ via the inverse DTFT.

### 2.2 Structured Logic — Step-by-Step Mental Framework

1. **Define the filter type** → Identify the passband and stopband in $[-\pi, \pi]$.
2. **Write $H(e^{j\omega})$** → Assign magnitude 1 in passband, 0 in stopband. Add linear phase term $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$ for causality-friendly shift.
3. **Apply Inverse DTFT** → Substitute $H(e^{j\omega})$ into the integral from $-\pi$ to $\pi$. Due to even symmetry, the integral can be split into two symmetrical parts.
4. **Evaluate the integral** → Use the standard result $\int e^{j\omega n} d\omega = \frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn}$.
5. **Simplify** → Use Euler's identity to arrive at a sinc-type closed-form expression.
6. **Handle $n = 0$ separately** → The sinc function is undefined at zero; apply L'Hôpital's rule to get $h(0) = \omega_c / \pi$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **#1 most-tested formula set** in KTU University Exams for Module 4. Memorize the impulse response expressions verbatim.

| Filter Type | Frequency Response $H(e^{j\omega})$ | Impulse Response $h(n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Ideal LPF** | $1$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$, $0$ otherwise | $\dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ for $n \ne 0$; $\dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$ for $n = 0$ |
| **Ideal HPF** | $0$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$, $1$ otherwise | $\delta(n) - \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ |
| **Ideal BPF** | $1$ for $\omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2$, $0$ otherwise | $\dfrac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} - \dfrac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}$ |
| **Ideal BSF** | $0$ for $\omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2$, $1$ otherwise | $\delta(n) - \dfrac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} + \dfrac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}$ |

| Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\omega_c$ | Cutoff frequency of LPF/HPF (rad/sample) |
| $\omega_1, \omega_2$ | Lower and upper cutoff frequencies of BPF/BSF |
| $n$ | Discrete time index, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| $\delta(n)$ | Unit impulse: $1$ at $n=0$, $0$ elsewhere |

### 2.4 Properties of the Ideal Filter Impulse Response

- **Infinite Duration**: $h(n) \ne 0$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Non-Causal**: $h(n)$ is non-zero for $n < 0$. A causal realization is impossible without truncation.
- **Symmetric (Even)**: $h(n) = h(-n)$ because the magnitude response is real and even.
- **Sinc-Shaped**: The ILPF impulse response is the discrete-time sinc function $\text{sinc}(\omega_c n / \pi)$.
- **Not Absolutely Summable**: $\sum \vert h(n) \vert$ diverges, so the system is **not BIBO stable** in the strict sense (oscillatory decay $\propto 1/n$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although ideal filters are unrealizable, they are indispensable for:

- **Filter design benchmarks**: The windowing method (Module 5) truncates $h(n)$ to obtain realizable FIR filters.
- **Spectral analysis**: Ideal BPFs isolate frequency bands for spectral measurement.
- **Communication systems**: Conceptually used to model channel selection in software-defined radio (SDR).
- **Image processing**: 2-D extensions (circularly symmetric ILPFs) are used for image smoothing, despite being implemented approximately.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impulse Response of the Ideal Low Pass Filter (ILPF)

**Step 1: Specify the ideal frequency response.**

$$
H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\
0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi
\end{cases}
$$

Here we ignore phase (assume zero phase for simplicity).

**Step 2: Apply the inverse DTFT definition.**

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the piecewise definition. The integrand is zero outside $[-\omega_c, \omega_c]$, so we narrow the limits.**

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} (1)\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral using $\int e^{j\omega n} d\omega = \dfrac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn}$.**

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute the limits.**

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \left( e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n} \right)
$$

**Step 6: Apply Euler's identity: $\sin(x) = \dfrac{e^{jx} - e^{-jx}}{2j}$.**

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \cdot 2j \sin(\omega_c n)
$$

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \ne 0
$$

**Step 7: Handle the special case $n = 0$ separately.** The expression $\frac{\sin(0)}{0}$ is of the indeterminate form $\frac{0}{0}$. Apply L'Hôpital's Rule with respect to $n$:

$$
\lim_{n \to 0} \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n} = \lim_{n \to 0} \frac{\omega_c \cos(\omega_c n)}{\pi} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}
$$

**Step 8: Combine cases into the final compact form.**

$$
h_{LP}(n) =
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = 0 \\
\dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, & n \ne 0
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Many students lose marks by forgetting the $n = 0$ case. Always write both branches explicitly.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Impulse Response of the Ideal High Pass Filter (IHPF)

**Step 1: Recognize the complementarity property.** The IHPF frequency response is the *complement* of the ILPF over $[-\pi, \pi]$:

$$
H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})
$$

This holds because in $[-\pi, \pi]$, the ILPF passes $[-\omega_c, \omega_c]$ and stops the rest, while the IHPF does the opposite. Their sum covers the entire band, weighted by 1.

**Step 2: Apply linearity of the inverse DTFT.**

$$
h_{HP}(n) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{1\} - \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})\}
$$

**Step 3: Compute each term.**

The inverse DTFT of the constant $H(e^{j\omega}) = 1$ is:

$$
h_{\text{all-pass}}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega = \delta(n)
$$

And the second term is just $h_{LP}(n)$ derived above.

**Step 4: Combine.**

$$
h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n) - h_{LP}(n)
$$

$$
h_{HP}(n) =
\begin{cases}
1 - \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = 0 \\
- \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, & n \ne 0
\end{cases}
$$

Equivalently, in unified form:

$$
h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n) - \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Impulse Response of the Ideal Band Pass Filter (IBPF)

**Step 1: Specify the frequency response.**

$$
H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2 \\
0, & \text{otherwise in } [-\pi, \pi]
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: Decompose into a difference of two ILPFs.** Observe that an IBPF with passband $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$ equals an ILPF with cutoff $\omega_2$ minus an ILPF with cutoff $\omega_1$:

$$
H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{LP, \omega_2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{LP, \omega_1}(e^{j\omega})
$$

**Step 3: Apply inverse DTFT and linearity.**

$$
h_{BP}(n) = h_{LP,\omega_2}(n) - h_{LP,\omega_1}(n)
$$

$$
h_{BP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} - \frac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \ne 0
$$

For $n = 0$:

$$
h_{BP}(0) = \frac{\omega_2}{\pi} - \frac{\omega_1}{\pi} = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\pi}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Impulse Response of the Ideal Band Stop Filter (IBSF)

**Step 1: Specify the frequency response.**

$$
H_{BS}(e^{j\omega}) =
\begin{cases}
0, & \omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2 \\
1, & \text{otherwise in } [-\pi, \pi]
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: Use the all-pass complement trick.** The BSF is the complement of the BPF:

$$
H_{BS}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - H_{BP}(e^{j\omega})
$$

**Step 3: Apply inverse DTFT.**

$$
h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n) - h_{BP}(n)
$$

$$
h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n) - \frac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} + \frac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \ne 0
$$

For $n = 0$:

$$
h_{BS}(0) = 1 - \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\pi} = \frac{\pi - \omega_2 + \omega_1}{\pi}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Zero-Phase Shifted Versions

To convert an ideal filter into a *causal* (delayed) form with linear phase, we multiply $H(e^{j\omega})$ by $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$, where $\alpha$ is the desired delay (typically chosen as $(N-1)/2$ for an $N$-tap FIR filter). This shifts $h(n) \to h(n - \alpha)$:

$$
h_{\text{causal}}(n) = h(n - \alpha)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This delay shift is the conceptual foundation of the **window method** for FIR filter design (covered in Module 5). Ideal filter impulse responses are *truncated* and *shifted* to obtain practical FIR filters.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Ideal Filter Realization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Frequency Specification H of e raised to j omega] --> B{Passband and Stopband defined?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Write Piecewise H of e raised to j omega over minus pi to pi]
    C --> D[Apply Inverse DTFT integral formula]
    D --> E[Integrate over Passband limits only]
    E --> F[Apply Euler Identity for sine]
    F --> G{Is n equals zero?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply LHospital Rule]
    G -- No --> I[Final sinc expression for n not zero]
    H --> J[Combine into Piecewise Closed Form h of n]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Add Linear Phase Shift e raised to minus j omega alpha]
    K --> L[Truncate to N taps using Window]
    L --> M[Realizable FIR Filter Coefficients]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Architecture — Decomposition of Ideal Filters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AP[All Pass H equals 1] --> SUB1[Subtract]
    LP1[Ideal LPF with cutoff omega 1] --> SUB1
    SUB1 --> HP1[Ideal HPF with cutoff omega 1]

    LP2[Ideal LPF with cutoff omega 2] --> SUB2[Subtract]
    LP3[Ideal LPF with cutoff omega 1] --> SUB2
    SUB2 --> BPF[Ideal BPF between omega 1 and omega 2]

    AP2[All Pass H equals 1] --> SUB3[Subtract]
    BPF2[Ideal BPF between omega 1 and omega 2] --> SUB3
    SUB3 --> BSF[Ideal BSF notch between omega 1 and omega 2]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Plot of ILPF Impulse Response (Sinc Function)

```
h_LP(n)
   |
ωc/π|        *           *           *
   |         |           |           |
   |    *    |    *      |    *      |    *
   |    |    |    |      |    |      |    |
---|----*----*----*------*----*------*----*------> n
   -4   -3   -2   -1     0    1     2    3    4
                  (Zero crossings at n = ±π/ωc, ±2π/ωc, ...)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The impulse response of an ILPF is a **discrete sinc function** centered at $n=0$. Zero crossings occur at $n = \pm k\pi / \omega_c$ for integer $k \ne 0$. The amplitude decays as $1/n$, never reaching zero — hence the filter is unrealizable with finite memory.

### 4.4 Side-by-Side Frequency Response Sketch

```
Magnitude |H(e^jω)|

ILPF:                    IHPF:
   |1 ___________           |1              ___________
   | |           |          | |             |           |
   | |           |          | |             |           |
___|_|___________|___      _|_|_____________|___________|___
  -π  -ωc  ωc   π           -π -ωc          ωc         π
       Pass|Stop               Stop|Pass

IBPF:                    IBSF:
   |1     _______           |1 ________     _______
   | |    |       |          | |       |    |       |
   | |    |       |          | |       |    |       |
___|_|____|_______|___      _|_|_______|____|_______|___
  -π  ω1  ω2      π          -π         ω1   ω2     π
       |Pass|                    |Pass|Stop|Pass|
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define an ideal discrete-time low pass filter. Write its frequency response specification.*

**Model Answer:**

An ideal discrete-time low pass filter (ILPF) is a theoretical filter that passes all frequency components below the cutoff frequency $\omega_c$ with unity gain and perfectly attenuates all frequencies above $\omega_c$ with zero gain, exhibiting a brick-wall magnitude response. Its frequency response is specified as:

$$
H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\
0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi
\end{cases}
$$

It has zero phase (or linear phase $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$) and is unrealizable due to its infinite, non-causal impulse response.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Why are ideal filters unrealizable in practice? Mention two reasons.*

**Model Answer:**

Ideal filters are unrealizable for the following reasons:

1. **Non-Causality**: The impulse response $h(n)$ of an ideal filter extends from $n = -\infty$ to $n = +\infty$, meaning the output at time $n$ depends on future input samples. No real-time physical system can satisfy this.

2. **Infinite Duration**: $h(n)$ is non-zero for all $n$, requiring infinite memory to implement. Truncation causes Gibbs phenomenon (ripples in the magnitude response).

Additionally, ideal filters are marginally not absolutely summable, which can lead to instability concerns in finite-precision implementations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the impulse response of an ideal low pass filter with cutoff frequency $\omega_c$. Show all integration steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ideal high pass filter has cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/3$. Compute $h_{HP}(0)$, $h_{HP}(1)$, $h_{HP}(2)$, and $h_{HP}(3)$. Comment on the symmetry. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The frequency response of the ILPF with cutoff $\omega_c$ is:

$$
H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\
0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi
\end{cases}
$$

Using inverse DTFT:

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega n} d\omega
$$

Evaluating:

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \left[ e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n} \right] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \ne 0
$$

For $n = 0$, applying L'Hôpital's Rule:

$$
h_{LP}(0) = \lim_{n \to 0} \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}
$$

Final answer:

$$
h_{LP}(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = 0 \\ \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, & n \ne 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

For $\omega_c = \pi/3$, the IHPF impulse response is:

$$
h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n) - \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}
$$

| $n$ | $\sin(\pi n/3)$ | $\sin(\pi n/3)/(\pi n)$ | $h_{HP}(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 (limit) | $\omega_c/\pi = 1/3$ | $1 - 1/3 = 2/3$ |
| 1 | $\sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$ | $\sqrt{3}/(2\pi)$ | $-\sqrt{3}/(2\pi) \approx -0.276$ |
| 2 | $\sin(2\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2$ | $\sqrt{3}/(4\pi)$ | $-\sqrt{3}/(4\pi) \approx -0.138$ |
| 3 | $\sin(\pi) = 0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

**Symmetry Comment:** The values are symmetric about $n = 0$ (i.e., $h_{HP}(-1) = h_{HP}(1)$), confirming that the impulse response is an **even function** due to the real, even magnitude response $H_{HP}(e^{j\omega})$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the impulse response of an ideal band pass filter with lower cutoff $\omega_1$ and upper cutoff $\omega_2$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ideal band stop filter has $\omega_1 = \pi/4$ and $\omega_2 = 3\pi/4$. Find $h_{BS}(0)$ and $h_{BS}(\pm 1)$. Also explain why this filter is unrealizable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The IBPF frequency response:

$$
H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2 \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Decompose into two ILPFs:

$$
H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{LP,\omega_2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{LP,\omega_1}(e^{j\omega})
$$

Applying inverse DTFT with linearity:

$$
h_{BP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} - \frac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \ne 0
$$

For $n = 0$:

$$
h_{BP}(0) = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\pi}
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

Using $\omega_1 = \pi/4$ and $\omega_2 = 3\pi/4$:

$$
h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n) - h_{BP}(n) = \delta(n) - \frac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} + \frac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}
$$

**Computing $h_{BS}(0)$:**

$$
h_{BS}(0) = 1 - \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\pi} = 1 - \frac{3\pi/4 - \pi/4}{\pi} = 1 - \frac{\pi/2}{\pi} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Computing $h_{BS}(1)$:**

$$
h_{BS}(1) = -\frac{\sin(3\pi/4)}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(\pi/4)}{\pi} = -\frac{\sqrt{2}/2}{\pi} + \frac{\sqrt{2}/2}{\pi} = 0
$$

**Computing $h_{BS}(-1)$:** By symmetry, $h_{BS}(-1) = h_{BS}(1) = 0$.

**Unrealizability:** This ideal band stop filter has an impulse response that extends from $n = -\infty$ to $n = +\infty$ with non-zero values (it is a sinc-type decay proportional to $1/n$). Therefore:
1. It is **non-causal** (values exist for $n < 0$).
2. It has **infinite memory** requirements.
3. Truncation introduces Gibbs phenomenon ripples in the stopband.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the $n = 0$ case**: The sinc expression $\sin(\omega_c n)/(\pi n)$ is **undefined** at $n=0$. Examiners frequently award 1 mark separately for applying L'Hôpital's Rule and reporting $h(0) = \omega_c / \pi$. Always write the final answer as a piecewise function.
> - **Sign error in IHPF**: The IHPF impulse response is $\delta(n) - \sin(\omega_c n)/(\pi n)$, NOT $\delta(n) + \sin(\omega_c n)/(\pi n)$. The negative sign is critical.
> - **Confusing BPF and BSF**: A common trap is swapping $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ in the formula. Verify which is the lower and upper cutoff before substituting.
> - **Missing the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor**: Some students forget the normalization constant in the inverse DTFT. The full integral must include $\frac{1}{2\pi}$.
> - **Linear phase omission**: For full marks on advanced problems, mention that adding a linear phase $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$ converts the zero-phase ideal filter into a causal-delayed FIR filter prototype.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition**: An ideal filter has a piecewise-constant (brick-wall) magnitude response and linear phase; it is unrealizable due to non-causality and infinite duration.
- **ILPF Impulse Response**: $h_{LP}(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ for $n \ne 0$; equals $\dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$ at $n = 0$.
- **IHPF Impulse Response**: $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n) - \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$, obtained via complementarity.
- **IBPF Impulse Response**: $h_{BP}(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} - \dfrac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}$.
- **IBSF Impulse Response**: $h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n) - \dfrac{\sin(\omega_2 n)}{\pi n} + \dfrac{\sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}$.
- **Inverse DTFT Formula**: $h(n) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$.
- **L'Hôpital's Rule**: Always needed for $n = 0$ case of any sinc expression.
- **Symmetry Property**: All ideal filter impulse responses are even functions: $h(n) = h(-n)$.
- **Amplitude Decay**: $\vert h(n) \vert \propto 1/n$ — algebraic decay, NOT exponential; hence truncation causes ripples.
- **Realizability Strategy**: To make an ideal filter realizable: (i) shift $h(n)$ by $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ for causality, (ii) truncate to $N$ taps, (iii) apply a tapered window (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) — this is the **window method** (Module 5).
- **Two-Minute Exam Memory Aid**: *"ILPF gives sinc; IHPF subtracts sinc from delta; IBPF is sinc-difference; IBSF is delta minus IBPF."*
- **Common Mark-Losing Errors**: Forgetting $n=0$ case, sign error in IHPF, missing $1/(2\pi)$, confusing BPF and BSF.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gibbs Phenomenon
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gibbs Phenomenon ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gibbs Phenomenon (Gibbs–Wilbraham Phenomenon):** When a signal containing a jump discontinuity is reconstructed using a *truncated* Fourier series (i.e., a finite number of harmonics), the partial-sum reconstruction exhibits persistent **oscillatory overshoots and undershoots** clustered around the point of discontinuity. The peak amplitude of these oscillations does **not** vanish as the number of terms $N \to \infty$; instead, it converges to a fixed value of approximately **8.95 %** of the magnitude of the jump. Although the *energy* of the ripples decreases (the oscillations become narrower), their *height* remains essentially constant. The phenomenon is also referred to as the **"ringing effect"** in discrete-time signal processing and FIR filter design, because truncated (windowed) impulse responses produce a reconstructed output that "rings" at sharp transitions in the input signal. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are an **artist** commissioned to draw a perfect step (a sudden vertical jump from value 0 to value 1). The rules say you can only use a limited number of smooth, gentle sinusoidal paint-strokes. Because you cannot use infinitely high frequency components, the moment you reach the vertical jump, your brush "overshoots" the line — it goes a little above 1, then a little below, then above again, oscillating with rapidly diminishing width. No matter how many strokes you add, that **first overshoot** stubbornly stays around **9 %** above the target. This stubborn overshoot is the Gibbs phenomenon. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in DSP?** Almost every practical FIR filter is built by *truncating* the impulse response of an *ideal* filter (which has an infinite, non-causal impulse response). The Gibbs phenomenon explains why every practical FIR filter has a finite **transition band** and finite **passband/stopband ripples** — they are mathematically inseparable from the act of truncation. ### 1.3 Where the Phenomenon Appears in KTU Syllabus The Gibbs phenomenon is centrally connected to three Module 4 topics: 1. **Truncation of Fourier series** — the act of dropping high-frequency harmonics. 2. **Windowing techniques** — multiplying the impulse response by a tapered window (Hamming, Hann, Blackman, Kaiser) to trade ripple amplitude for transition-band width. 3. **FIR filter design by the window method** — the ripples you see in the frequency response of a windowed FIR filter are a direct, *controllable* manifestation of the Gibbs phenomenon. > [!TIP] > **Mental model for exams:** *More abrupt the truncation (rectangular window) → taller the ripples (≈ 9 %).* *Smoother the window → shorter the ripples, but wider the transition band.* This trade-off is the heart of window-method FIR design. ### 1.4 Key Quantitative Metric The canonical **overshoot ratio** for a unit-step discontinuity is $$ \text{Overshoot} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) \;\approx\; 0.08949 \;\equiv\; \mathbf{8.949\%} $$ where $\mathrm{Si}(x) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}\frac{\sin t}{t}\,dt$ is the **Sine Integral** function, with $\mathrm{Si}(\pi) \approx 1.8519370$. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Origin — Why Does Gibbs Phenomenon Occur? The phenomenon arises from a fundamental mathematical truth: > A discontinuous function in the **time domain** requires **infinite bandwidth** in the frequency domain. A practical system (or a Fourier-series partial sum) can only retain a *finite* number of frequency components. Truncating the spectrum is mathematically equivalent to multiplying the ideal infinite-duration impulse response by a **rectangular window** in the time domain. Multiplication in time ↔ **convolution** in frequency. The frequency response of a rectangular window is the **Dirichlet kernel**, which has a main lobe of width $2\pi/N$ and **side lobes** that decay only as $1/n$. These side lobes are the spectral fingerprints of the time-domain ripples we call the Gibbs phenomenon. ### 2.2 Logical Step Decomposition **Step 1 — Express the truncated Fourier series as a convolution.** The $N$-term partial sum of a periodic signal $x(t)$ with fundamental period $T$ can be written as $$ x_N(t) \;=\; \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(\tau)\,D_N(t-\tau)\,d\tau $$ where $D_N(\cdot)$ is the **Dirichlet kernel** of order $N$. **Step 2 — Identify the Dirichlet kernel structure.** $$ D_N(\theta) \;=\; \frac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\theta\right]}{\sin(\theta/2)} $$ **Step 3 — Recognize the side-lobe behavior.** The Dirichlet kernel is **even**, has unit area, oscillates with period $2\pi/N$, and its side lobes decay only as $1/n$. The first side-lobe peak occurs near $\theta \approx \pm 3\pi/(2N+1)$ and has magnitude $$ \max_{\theta \neq 0}\bigl\vert D_N(\theta) \bigr\vert \;\approx\; \frac{2}{(2N+1)\,\sin(3\pi/(2(2N+1)))} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.08949 \cdot (2N+1)} $$ **Step 4 — Scale with the jump height.** When convolved with a step of height $A$, the *relative* overshoot is the side-lobe area (a constant), and the *absolute* overshoot equals approximately $0.08949 \times A$, independent of $N$. **Step 5 — Observe the asymptotic behaviour.** As $N$ increases, the main lobe of $D_N$ narrows proportionally to $1/N$, so the *width* of the ringing region shrinks. But the *height* of the first overshoot, being the integral of the side-lobe under a step, asymptotes to a constant — the **Gibbs constant**. **Step 6 — Connect to windowing.** Replacing the rectangular truncation with a smoother window (Hann, Hamming, Blackman, Kaiser) is equivalent to *convolving the Dirichlet kernel with the window's frequency response*, which attenuates the side lobes and hence the ripple amplitude, while broadening the main lobe and hence the transition band. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every formula** you must memorize for Gibbs-phenomenon problems in KTU ESE. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute value (to preserve markdown-table integrity). | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Truncated Fourier series | $x_N(t) = \sum_{k=-N}^{N} c_k\,e^{jk\omega_0 t}$ | The $N$-term reconstruction of a periodic signal | | 2 | Dirichlet kernel (CT) | $D_N(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\theta\right]}{\sin(\theta/2)}$ | Frequency-domain signature of rectangular truncation | | 3 | Dirichlet kernel (DT) | $D_N(\omega) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\omega\right]}{\sin(\omega/2)}$ | Discrete-time counterpart on $[-\pi,\pi]$ | | 4 | Convolution form | $x_N(t) = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(\tau)\,D_N(t-\tau)\,d\tau$ | Reveals why ripples appear at discontinuities | | 5 | Gibbs overshoot (relative) | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\!\frac{\sin t}{t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\pi}\mathrm{Si}(\pi) \approx 0.08949$ | The fundamental **8.949 %** constant | | 6 | Sine integral definition | $\mathrm{Si}(x) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{x}\dfrac{\sin t}{t}\,dt$ | Appears in overshoot formula | | 7 | Side-lobe peak (approx.) | $\mathrm{SL}_\text{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{\sin(3\pi/2)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N+1/2} \cdot (2N+1) = 0.217\,N$ | Scales the absolute overshoot | | 8 | First overshoot location | $t^* \approx \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_0\,(N+1/2)}$ | Narrows as $N$ grows | | 9 | Windowed truncation | $x_N^{(w)}(t) = \sum_{k=-N}^{N} c_k\,W_k\,e^{jk\omega_0 t}$ | $W_k$ are window coefficients | | 10 | Hamming window ripple | $\delta_p \approx -53\,\mathrm{dB}$, $\delta_s \approx -31\,\mathrm{dB}$ | Trade-off: lower ripple, wider transition | | 11 | Blackman window ripple | $\delta_p \approx -74\,\mathrm{dB}$ | Even smoother response | | 12 | Kaiser window parameter $\beta$ | $\beta = \begin{cases} 0.1102(A-8.7) & A>50 \\ 0.5842(A-21)^{0.4}+0.07886(A-21) & 21\le A\le 50 \\ 0 & A<21 \end{cases}$ | $A = -20\log_{10}\delta$ is the attenuation in dB | | 13 | Rectangular-window ripple | $\delta_p = \delta_s \approx 8.949\,\%$ | Baseline case ($N \to \infty$) | | 14 | Transition bandwidth | $\Delta\omega \approx \dfrac{c\,\omega_s}{N}$ | $c$ depends on the window (Hann: 3.1, Hamming: 3.3, Blackman: 5.5) | ### 2.4 Engineering Significance | Field | Application of Gibbs Phenomenon | |---|---| | **Audio DSP** | Why naive square-wave synthesis produces audible "click" pre-echos; explains the need for band-limited synthesis. | | **Image Processing** | Ringing artifacts around sharp edges after ideal low-pass filtering (e.g., JPEG block boundaries). | | **Communication Systems** | Eye-diagram overshoot in band-limited digital links; ISI caused by filter truncation. | | **FIR Filter Design** | The passband/stopband ripples of a windowed FIR filter *are* a controlled Gibbs phenomenon. | | **Antenna Arrays** | Sidelobe levels in uniformly weighted aperture arrays — the *spatial* Gibbs phenomenon. | | **Numerical PDEs** | Spectral methods exhibit Gibbs-like oscillations (spurious "wiggles") near shock waves. | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overshoot of Truncated Square-Wave Fourier Series **Problem Setup.** Consider the *odd* periodic square wave of fundamental period $2\pi$ and amplitude $A$: $$ x(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} +A, & 0 < t < \pi \\ -A, & -\pi < t < 0 \end{cases} $$ The complex Fourier-series coefficients are $$ c_k \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} x(t)\,e^{-jk t}\,dt $$ **Step 1 — Compute $c_k$.** Because $x(t)$ is real and odd, $c_k$ is purely imaginary. Direct evaluation: $$ \begin{aligned} c_k &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} A\,e^{-jk t}\,dt \;-\; \int_{-\pi}^{0} A\,e^{-jk t}\,dt\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{2\pi}\left[\frac{e^{-jk t}}{-jk}\Big|_{0}^{\pi} \;-\; \frac{e^{-jk t}}{-jk}\Big|_{-\pi}^{0}\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{-2\pi j k}\left[(e^{-jk\pi}-1) \;-\; (1-e^{jk\pi})\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{-2\pi j k}\left[e^{-jk\pi}+e^{jk\pi}-2\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{-2\pi j k}\left[2\cos(k\pi)-2\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{\pi j k}\left[1-\cos(k\pi)\right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Use $\cos(k\pi) = (-1)^k$.** $$ c_k \;=\; \frac{A}{\pi j k}\bigl[1-(-1)^k\bigr] $$ For $k$ even, $1-(-1)^k = 0$ (DC and even harmonics vanish). For $k$ odd, $1-(-1)^k = 2$: $$ c_k \;=\; \begin{cases} \dfrac{2A}{\pi j k}, & k \text{ odd} \\ 0, & k \text{ even} \\ 0, & k=0 \end{cases} $$ **Step 3 — Reconstruct via the truncated series.** Using $c_{-k} = c_k^* = -2A/(\pi j k)$ for $k$ odd, the real trigonometric form is $$ x_N(t) \;=\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\!\bigl[(2m-1)t\bigr]}{2m-1} $$ **Step 4 — Find the first maximum of $x_N(t)$ near $t=0^+$.** Differentiate: $$ \frac{d x_N}{dt} \;=\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\cos\!\bigl[(2m-1)t\bigr] \;=\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin(2Nt)}{2\sin t} $$ The derivative is zero when $\sin(2Nt)=0$ and $\sin t \neq 0$, i.e., at $t_p = p\pi/(2N)$ for integer $p$. The first such extremum **after** the jump at $t=0$ is $$ t_1 \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2N} $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate $x_N(t_1)$.** $$ x_N\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right) \;=\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\!\left[\dfrac{(2m-1)\pi}{2N}\right]}{2m-1} $$ **Step 6 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.** Substitute $\theta_m = (2m-1)\pi/(2N)$ and let $N \to \infty$ so that the sum becomes a Riemann integral: $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to\infty} x_N\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right) &= \frac{4A}{\pi}\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\!\bigl[(2m-1)\pi/(2N)\bigr]}{2m-1} \\ &= \frac{4A}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\pi}{2N}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\!\bigl[(2m-1)\pi/(2N)\bigr]}{(2m-1)\pi/(2N)}\cdot\frac{1}{(2m-1)\pi/(2N)} \cdot \frac{(2m-1)\pi}{2N} \\ \end{aligned} $$ A cleaner route uses the identity $$ \sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\!\bigl[(2m-1)x\bigr]}{2m-1} \;\xrightarrow{N\to\infty}\; \frac{\pi}{4}, \quad 0 < x < \pi $$ so as $N \to \infty$ with $x = \pi/(2N) \to 0$: $$ \lim_{N\to\infty} x_N\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right) \;=\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du \;=\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) $$ **Step 7 — Numerical evaluation.** $$ \mathrm{Si}(\pi) \;=\; \int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du \;\approx\; 1.8519370 $$ $$ \frac{4A}{\pi}\,(1.8519370) \;\approx\; 2.3578\,A $$ **Step 8 — Compute the relative overshoot.** The true value of the signal at $t \to 0^+$ is $+A$. The overshoot is $$ \text{Absolute overshoot} \;=\; 2.3578A - A \;=\; 1.3578A $$ But the **relative overshoot** is conventionally normalized to half the total jump height $2A$: $$ \text{Relative overshoot} \;=\; \frac{1.3578A}{2A} \;\approx\; 0.6789 $$ This **0.6789** is the overshoot measured from the *midpoint* of the jump (which is 0), i.e., the reconstruction reaches about **67.89 %** of the way from the low value $-A$ to the high value $+A$ on the *first* overshoot, instead of stopping at the midpoint. The standard 8.949 % figure arises when the overshoot is instead normalized to the *upper* value $+A$: $$ \frac{2.3578A - A}{A} \;=\; 1.3578 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 0.3578 \times 2 \;\to\; \text{re-stated as } \frac{4A}{\pi}\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - 1 $$ More precisely, the **canonical Gibbs constant** (overshoot *above* the upper level, normalized by the jump magnitude $2A$) is $$ \boxed{\;\eta \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) \;=\; \frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du \;\approx\; 1.17898\;-\;1 \;\approx\; 0.08949\;} $$ That is, the peak reconstruction reaches $$ A\bigl(1 + 0.08949\bigr) \;=\; 1.08949\,A $$ which is **8.949 %** above the asymptotic level $A$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overshoot as a Convolution with the Dirichlet Kernel **Goal.** Show that the overshoot is *independent* of $N$ as $N \to \infty$ by examining the convolution integral. **Step 1 — Periodic convolution form.** The partial sum can be expressed as $$ x_N(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} x(\tau)\,D_N(t-\tau)\,d\tau $$ **Step 2 — Specialise to a step discontinuity of height $A$ at $t=0$.** Let $x(t) = A\,u(t)$ on $[-\pi,\pi]$ (with the periodic continuation). Then $$ x_N(t) \;=\; \frac{A}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} D_N(t-\tau)\,d\tau \;=\; \frac{A}{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)(t-\tau)\right]}{\sin\!\left[\tfrac{1}{2}(t-\tau)\right)}\,d\tau $$ **Step 3 — Change variable to $u = (t-\tau)$, reverse limits.** For $t > 0$: $$ x_N(t) \;=\; \frac{A}{2\pi}\int_{t-\pi}^{t} D_N(u)\,du $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the Dirichlet kernel and use the integral identity.** $$ \begin{aligned} x_N(t) &= \frac{A}{2\pi}\int_{t-\pi}^{t}\frac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)u\right]}{\sin(u/2)}\,du \end{aligned} $$ For $t \to 0^+$ and large $N$, the integrand oscillates rapidly except where $u$ is small. We may replace $\sin(u/2) \approx u/2$ in the small-$u$ region, giving the well-known *sinc*-approximation. The indefinite integral of $\mathrm{sinc}$ yields the Sine Integral: $$ \int_{0}^{x}\frac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)u\right]}{u}\,du \;=\; \mathrm{Si}\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)x\right] $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the maximum.** The first maximum of $x_N(t)$ occurs near $t \approx \pi/(2N+1)$, where the upper limit of the integral in Step 3 still places the main lobe of $D_N$ entirely inside the integration domain. At this $t$, $$ \lim_{N\to\infty}\,x_N(t^*) \;=\; \frac{A}{2} + \frac{A}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) $$ **Step 6 — Identify the overshoot.** The signal's asymptotic value just after the jump is $A$. Therefore the overshoot is $$ \frac{A}{2} + \frac{A}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - A \;=\; \frac{A}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - \frac{A}{2} $$ Normalized to the jump magnitude $A$ (from the midpoint $A/2$ to the upper level $A$): $$ \text{overshoot} \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - \frac{1}{2} $$ Wait — re-examination shows the *standard* statement is that the overshoot reaches the level $$ \boxed{\;\frac{A}{2} + \frac{2A}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du \;-\; A \;=\; \frac{A}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - \frac{A}{2} \;\approx\; 0.08949\,A\;} $$ above the upper plateau. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Discrete-Time Equivalent (DTFT Truncation) **Goal.** Demonstrate that the same 8.95 % overshoot applies to discrete-time signals whose DTFT is truncated to $\vert\omega\vert \le \pi/N$. **Step 1 — Ideal low-pass filter impulse response.** The ideal discrete-time low-pass filter with cutoff $\omega_c$ has impulse response $$ h_d[n] \;=\; \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\omega_c n} \;=\; \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \neq 0, \qquad h_d[0] \;=\; \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} $$ **Step 2 — Truncate to length $2N+1$.** Multiply by a rectangular window: $$ h[n] \;=\; h_d[n]\,w_R[n], \quad w_R[n] \;=\; 1 \text{ for } -N \le n \le N $$ **Step 3 — Compute the magnitude response near $\omega_c$.** Standard text-book result: $$ H(\omega) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(\theta)\,W_R(\omega-\theta)\,d\theta $$ For $\omega$ near $\omega_c$, $H_d(\theta)$ is a step of height 1, and the convolution reduces to the integral of the Dirichlet kernel over the upper half-band. The analysis from §3.2 carries through verbatim, giving the same asymptotic overshoot of $\approx 8.949\,\%$ in the magnitude response of a *rectangular-windowed* FIR low-pass filter. **Step 4 — Interpretation.** Therefore, the design specifications of any windowed FIR filter — passband ripple $\delta_p$, stopband attenuation $\delta_s$, and transition bandwidth $\Delta\omega$ — are *all* consequences of the Gibbs phenomenon plus the choice of window. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Square-Wave Reconstruction **Question.** Reconstruct the square wave $x(t) = \mathrm{sgn}(\sin t)$ using $N=5$, $N=10$, and $N=25$ harmonics. Compute the value of the partial sum at the first overshoot peak $t = \pi/(2N)$. **Solution Table.** | $N$ | $t^* = \pi/(2N)$ | $\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin[(2m-1)t^*]}{2m-1}$ | $x_N(t^*)$ (units of $A$) | Overshoot % | |---|---|---|---|---| | 5 | $\pi/10 \approx 0.3142$ | $0.1811$ | $1.1083$ | $10.83\,\%$ | | 10 | $\pi/20 \approx 0.1571$ | $0.1787$ | $1.0933$ | $9.33\,\%$ | | 25 | $\pi/50 \approx 0.0628$ | $0.1783$ | $1.0901$ | $9.01\,\%$ | | 100 | $\pi/200 \approx 0.0157$ | $0.1782$ | $1.08949$ | $8.95\,\%$ | | $\infty$ | $0^+$ | $\frac{\pi}{4}\cdot\frac{4}{\pi}\mathrm{Si}(\pi)/\mathrm{Si}(\pi) = \mathrm{Si}(\pi)/\pi$ | $1.08949$ | $8.949\,\%$ | **Observation.** The overshoot converges *rapidly* — by $N=25$ it is already within 0.1 % of its asymptotic value. The location $t^*$ shrinks as $1/N$, so the ringing region becomes tighter and tighter. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 4.
