KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]** > Define the Gibbs phenomenon. State the asymptotic overshoot value and explain why it does not vanish as $N \to \infty$. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Gibbs phenomenon** refers to the persistent oscillatory overshoot and undershoot observed in the partial-sum reconstruction of a signal containing a jump discontinuity, when reconstructed using a truncated Fourier series. The **asymptotic overshoot** is approximately **8.949 %** of the jump magnitude, independent of the number of terms $N$ used in the reconstruction. **Why it does not vanish:** The overshoot arises from the **side-lobe structure** of the Dirichlet kernel $D_N(\theta)$, which has a first side-lobe peak height of approximately $0.08949 \cdot (2N+1)$ but a side-lobe area (i.e., the integral of the first side-lobe) that converges to a *constant* as $N \to \infty$. Hence the relative overshoot — being an integral property — does not diminish, although the *width* of the overshoot region shrinks proportionally to $1/N$. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation tip:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for stating the 8.949 % figure, and 1 mark for the explanation involving the Dirichlet kernel. A bare statement of "8.95 %" without the "why" will lose the third mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** > What is the role of windowing functions in mitigating the Gibbs phenomenon? Name any two commonly used windows and state one trade-off involved. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of windowing:** Multiplying the truncated impulse response (or Fourier series) by a *smooth* window function (such as Hamming, Hann, Blackman, or Kaiser) is equivalent to convolving the Dirichlet kernel with the window's frequency response. This **suppresses the side lobes** of the Dirichlet kernel, thereby reducing the amplitude of the Gibbs ripples. However, it simultaneously **broadens the main lobe**, leading to a wider transition band. **Two commonly used windows:** 1. **Hamming window** — provides a stopband attenuation of approximately **53 dB**. 2. **Blackman window** — provides a stopband attenuation of approximately **74 dB**. **Trade-off:** A smoother window (e.g., Blackman) reduces ripple amplitude at the cost of a **wider transition band** — i.e., we trade *ripple* for *sharpness of cut-off*. There is no window that simultaneously minimises both. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Fourier Series Analysis Path* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]** > **(a)** Consider the periodic square wave > $$x(t) = \begin{cases} +A, & 0 < t < \pi \\ -A, & -\pi < t < 0 \end{cases}, \quad x(t+2\pi) = x(t)$$ > Derive the complex Fourier series coefficients and show that > $$c_k = \frac{2A}{j\pi k}\bigl[1-(-1)^k\bigr] \quad (k \neq 0).$$ > **[(7 marks)]** > > **(b)** The truncated Fourier series reconstruction using the first $N$ odd harmonics is > $$x_N(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\bigl[(2m-1)t\bigr]}{2m-1}.$$ > Determine the location $t^*$ of the first overshoot peak in $(0,\pi)$, and show that > $$\lim_{N\to\infty} x_N(t^*) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du = \frac{4A}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi).$$ > Hence, calculate the **percentage overshoot** and identify the **Gibbs constant**. **[(7 marks)]** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) — Derivation of Fourier coefficients [7 marks]** The complex Fourier-series coefficient is defined as $$ c_k \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{T} x(t)\,e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\,dt, \qquad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T} = 1 $$ With $T = 2\pi$: $$ \begin{aligned} c_k &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} A\,e^{-jk t}\,dt - \int_{-\pi}^{0} A\,e^{-jk t}\,dt\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{2\pi}\left[\frac{e^{-jk t}}{-jk}\bigg|_{0}^{\pi} - \frac{e^{-jk t}}{-jk}\bigg|_{-\pi}^{0}\right] \\ &= \frac{A}{2\pi(-jk)}\bigl[(e^{-jk\pi} - 1) - (1 - e^{jk\pi})\bigr] \\ &= \frac{A}{-2\pi jk}\bigl[2\cos(k\pi) - 2\bigr] \\ &= \frac{A}{\pi jk}\bigl[1 - \cos(k\pi)\bigr] \\ &= \frac{A}{\pi jk}\bigl[1 - (-1)^k\bigr] \end{aligned} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2: $$ \boxed{\;c_k = \frac{2A}{j\pi k}\bigl[1 - (-1)^k\bigr]\;}, \qquad k \neq 0 $$ For $k = 0$: $$ c_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\int_{0}^{\pi} A\,dt - \int_{-\pi}^{0} A\,dt\right] = \frac{1}{2\pi}[\pi A + \pi A] = A $$ But because $x(t)$ is odd about $t=0$ in the sense that the average value over a period is zero, we recognise that the *DC component* in the symmetric periodic extension about the midpoint of the jump is zero; the *form* $A$ above arises only if we consider a half-wave-rectified interpretation. For the odd square wave, $c_0 = 0$ by symmetry. ✅ **Part (b) — Overshoot computation [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Differentiate $x_N(t)$ to locate extrema.** $$ \frac{dx_N}{dt} = \frac{4A}{\pi}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\cos[(2m-1)t] $$ Using the identity $\sum_{m=1}^{N}\cos[(2m-1)t] = \dfrac{\sin(2Nt)}{2\sin t}$: $$ \frac{dx_N}{dt} = \frac{4A}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sin(2Nt)}{2\sin t} = \frac{2A\sin(2Nt)}{\pi\sin t} $$ **Step 2 — Set the derivative to zero.** The first zero of $\sin(2Nt)$ for $t \in (0,\pi)$ is at $$ t^* = \frac{\pi}{2N} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate $x_N(t^*)$.** $$ x_N\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\!\left[\dfrac{(2m-1)\pi}{2N}\right]}{2m-1} $$ **Step 4 — Take the limit using a Riemann sum.** Set $\theta_m = (2m-1)\pi/(2N)$, so $\Delta\theta = \pi/N$. Then $$ \begin{aligned} \lim_{N\to\infty}x_N(t^*) &= \frac{4A}{\pi}\lim_{N\to\infty}\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{\sin\theta_m}{\theta_m}\cdot\frac{\Delta\theta}{1} \\ &= \frac{4A}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du \end{aligned} $$ The integral $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du = \mathrm{Si}(\pi)$ is the **Sine Integral** evaluated at $\pi$, with numerical value $\mathrm{Si}(\pi) \approx 1.8519370$. **Step 5 — Compute the overshoot percentage.** $$ x_N(t^*) \;\approx\; \frac{4A}{\pi}\cdot 1.8519370 \;\approx\; 2.3578\,A $$ The true upper plateau is $A$. The overshoot is $$ \text{Overshoot} = 2.3578A - A = 1.3578A $$ Normalized to the jump magnitude $2A$ (the *standard* normalization): $$ \text{Relative overshoot} = \frac{1.3578A}{2A} = 0.6789 \;\Rightarrow\; 67.89\,\% $$ This means the reconstruction overshoots to about **67.89 % above the midpoint** on the first peak. Equivalently, the overshoot *above the upper plateau* is $$ \frac{1.3578A}{A} - 1 = 0.3578 \;\text{(i.e., 35.78 % above }A\text{)} $$ Wait — recheck by the canonical normalization. The **canonical Gibbs constant** is defined as $$ \eta \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) \;\approx\; \frac{1.8519370}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.58949 $$ Actually, the **standard** statement is that the maximum of $x_N(t)$ as $N \to \infty$ equals $$ A + 0.08949\,A = 1.08949\,A $$ The factor $0.08949$ is the **Gibbs overshoot constant**, which arises as $$ 0.08949 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du - \frac{1}{2} = 0.58949 - 0.50000 = 0.08949 $$ So the **Gibbs constant** is $$ \boxed{\;\eta = \frac{1}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - \frac{1}{2} \approx 0.08949 \;\equiv\; 8.949\,\%\;} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students frequently confuse the **overshoot above the plateau** (≈ 8.949 %) with the **overshoot above the midpoint** (≈ 67.89 %) or the **maximum value of the partial sum** (≈ 1.08949 in normalised units). Be very explicit about your **normalisation convention** — write the formula $\eta = \frac{1}{\pi}\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - \frac{1}{2}$ before plugging in numbers. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Many students incorrectly assert that the overshoot *decreases* with $N$. It does *not*. The *width* decreases, but the *height* asymptotes. State this explicitly in the conclusion. --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *FIR Filter Design Path* **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4 — Alternative]** > **(a)** The ideal discrete-time low-pass filter has impulse response > $$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad n \neq 0, \qquad h_d[0] = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi},$$ > where $\omega_c$ is the cut-off frequency. To design a practical FIR filter of length $2N+1$, we truncate using a rectangular window $w_R[n]$ for $-N \le n \le N$. > **(i)** Write the expression for the practical filter $h[n]$. > **(ii)** Show that the frequency response of the practical filter is the convolution of the ideal response with the Dirichlet kernel. Hence, identify the source of the Gibbs phenomenon in FIR filter design. **[(7 marks)]** > > **(b)** The passband and stopband ripples of a rectangular-windowed FIR filter are both approximately equal to the **Gibbs overshoot constant**. Design a low-pass FIR filter with the following specifications using a **Hamming window**: > - Passband edge: $\omega_p = 0.4\pi$ > - Stopband edge: $\omega_s = 0.6\pi$ > - Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 50\,\mathrm{dB}$ > Determine the filter order $N$ and the cut-off frequency $\omega_c$. Justify why the Hamming window satisfies the attenuation requirement while a rectangular window does not. **[(7 marks)]** --- #### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION **Part (a) [7 marks]** **(i)** The practical FIR filter of length $2N+1$ is obtained by point-wise multiplication of the ideal impulse response with a rectangular window: $$ \boxed{\;h[n] = h_d[n]\,w_R[n] = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, & -N \le n \le N,\; n \neq 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = 0 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\;} $$ **(ii)** Multiplication in the discrete-time domain corresponds to **periodic convolution** in the discrete-frequency domain (DTFT): $$ H(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(\theta)\,W_R(\omega - \theta)\,d\theta $$ The DTFT of the rectangular window is the **Dirichlet kernel**: $$ W_R(\omega) = \sum_{n=-N}^{N}e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\omega\right]}{\sin(\omega/2)} = D_N(\omega) $$ The ideal low-pass response $H_d(\omega)$ is a rectangle of height 1 on $[-\omega_c,\omega_c]$ and zero elsewhere. The convolution of this rectangle with $D_N(\omega)$ produces: - A **main lobe** of width $\approx 2\pi/(2N+1)$ centred at $\omega = 0$. - **Side lobes** that decay as $1/\omega$ and produce the ripples in both passband and stopband. **Source of Gibbs phenomenon:** The side lobes of the Dirichlet kernel, convolved with the sharp transition of $H_d(\omega)$, produce the persistent overshoot/undershoot ripples — this **is** the Gibbs phenomenon, now appearing in the *frequency response* of the filter. Just as in time-domain Fourier reconstruction, the side-lobe area (a constant) dictates a fixed relative ripple amplitude of approximately **8.949 %**, regardless of $N$. **Part (b) [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Required filter order $N$.** For a Hamming window, the empirical formula (Rabiner–Gold) is $$ N \approx \frac{3.3}{(\omega_s - \omega_p)/\pi} = \frac{3.3\pi}{\omega_s - \omega_p} $$ Substituting: $$ N \approx \frac{3.3\pi}{0.6\pi - 0.4\pi} = \frac{3.3\pi}{0.2\pi} = \frac{3.3}{0.2} = 16.5 $$ Round up to the next integer: $N = 17$. The filter length is $2N+1 = 35$ taps. **Step 2 — Cut-off frequency $\omega_c$.** The cut-off is taken at the midpoint of the transition band: $$ \boxed{\;\omega_c = \frac{\omega_p + \omega_s}{2} = \frac{0.4\pi + 0.6\pi}{2} = 0.5\pi\;} $$ **Step 3 — Why Hamming suffices.** The stopband attenuation of a rectangular-windowed FIR filter is limited by the first side-lobe of the Dirichlet kernel, which is approximately **−21 dB** (= $20\log_{10}(0.08949 \cdot \text{stuff})$). This is far below the required 50 dB. The Hamming window, by tapering the truncation, suppresses the side lobes to approximately **−53 dB**, comfortably exceeding the 50 dB requirement. The cost is a slightly wider transition band (a factor of $\approx 3.3/0.9 \approx 3.67$ times wider than the rectangular case for the same $N$), which is acceptable given the specifications. **Step 4 — Summary of design.** | Specification | Value | |---|---| | Filter type | Low-pass FIR, linear phase, Type I symmetric | | Length | $M = 2N+1 = 35$ taps | | Window | Hamming | | Cut-off $\omega_c$ | $0.5\pi$ | | Passband edge $\omega_p$ | $0.4\pi$ | | Stopband edge $\omega_s$ | $0.6\pi$ | | Transition band $\Delta\omega$ | $0.2\pi$ | | Passband ripple $\delta_p$ | $\approx 0.0194\,\%$ | | Stopband attenuation $A_s$ | $\approx 53\,\mathrm{dB}$ ✅ (exceeds 50 dB) | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Students often use the *rectangular*-window formula ($N \approx 0.9\pi/(\omega_s-\omega_p)$) when in fact a smoother window is required. Always verify that the chosen window's stopband attenuation meets the spec. The *minimum* order for a given attenuation $A$ (dB) is given by the Kaiser formula > $$N \approx \frac{A - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega}$$ > Use this for Kaiser-window designs. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #4:** A common error is to set $\omega_c = \omega_p$ or $\omega_c = \omega_s$. The cut-off of the *ideal* response (which is the centre of the transition band) is at the *midpoint* $(\omega_p + \omega_s)/2$. The passband and stopband edges of the *practical* filter then fall on either side of $\omega_c$ symmetrically only for symmetric windows; in general, the exact positions are determined by the actual frequency response after windowing. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gibbs Phenomenon** - **Definition:** Persistent overshoot/undershoot in truncated Fourier series reconstructions of discontinuous signals. - **Canonical overshoot:** $\eta = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\,\mathrm{Si}(\pi) - \dfrac{1}{2} \approx \mathbf{8.949\,\%}$. - **Width behaviour:** Oscillation width $\propto 1/N$ — shrinks as $N$ grows. - **Height behaviour:** Oscillation height $\to$ constant as $N \to \infty$ — does **not** vanish. - **Root cause:** Side lobes of the **Dirichlet kernel** $D_N(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left[\left(N+\tfrac{1}{2}\right)\theta\right]}{\sin(\theta/2)}$. - **Pointwise convergence:** Fails at every jump discontinuity. - **$L^2$ convergence:** Preserved (Parseval's theorem holds; total ripple energy $\to 0$). - **Rectangular window:** $\delta_p = \delta_s \approx 8.949\,\%$, transition band narrowest, $A_s \approx 21\,\mathrm{dB}$. - **Hann window:** $\delta_p \approx 0.055\,\%$, $A_s \approx 44\,\mathrm{dB}$, transition band $\approx 3.1\pi/N$. - **Hamming window:** $\delta_p \approx 0.019\,\%$, $A_s \approx 53\,\mathrm{dB}$, transition band $\approx 3.3\pi/N$. - **Blackman window:** $\delta_p \approx 0.0017\,\%$, $A_s \approx 74\,\mathrm{dB}$, transition band $\approx 5.5\pi/N$. - **Kaiser window:** Tunable $\beta$ parameter; provides optimal trade-off for a given $A_s$. - **Filter-order formula (Hamming):** $N \approx \dfrac{3.3\pi}{\omega_s - \omega_p}$. - **Filter-order formula (Kaiser):** $N \approx \dfrac{A_s - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega}$. - **First overshoot location:** $t^* = \dfrac{\pi}{2N\omega_0}$ for the time-domain square wave; analogously $\omega^* = \omega_c \pm \pi/(2N+1)$ for the frequency response of a low-pass filter. - **Sine Integral value (memorize):** $\mathrm{Si}(\pi) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\pi}\frac{\sin u}{u}\,du \approx 1.8519370$. - **Key insight:** *Truncation in time = convolution in frequency*; *truncation in frequency = convolution in time*. Either way, Gibbs appears. - **Engineering applications:** Audio click removal, image de-ringing, antenna sidelobe control, FIR filter design, numerical PDE solvers. - **Historical note:** Discovered by Henry Wilbraham (1848), rediscovered by Albert Michelson (1898), explained by Josiah Willard Gibbs (1899). Hence the dual name **Gibbs–Wilbraham phenomenon**. - **One-line exam answer:** *"Gibbs phenomenon: finite-bandwidth reconstruction of a jump discontinuity produces overshoots of ~8.95 % that persist in height but shrink in width as bandwidth grows."* ---

topics.title: Fixed Window Functions: Rectangular, Hamming, Blackman
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed Window Functions: Rectangular, Hamming, Blackman

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Fixed Window Functions in FIR Filter Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Window Function** (or simply a *window*) is a finite-duration, real-valued, even-symmetric sequence $w[n]$ of length $N = M+1$ that is multiplied (point-by-point) with the *ideal* infinite-duration impulse response $h_d[n]$ of a desired filter. This multiplication truncates the ideal response to a finite length, thereby yielding a **practical, realizable FIR filter** $h[n]$.

Mathematically, the windowed impulse response is:
$$h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad 0 \le n \le M$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "A window function is a mathematical function of finite duration that is zero-valued outside of some chosen interval. When multiplied by an ideal filter's impulse response, it produces a finite-length causal FIR filter."

The **Three Fixed (or Standard) Windows** prescribed in the KTU PCICT601 syllabus are:
1. **Rectangular Window** (the trivial/truncation window)
2. **Hamming Window** (raised cosine variant)
3. **Blackman Window** (double-cosine variant)

Each fixed window is characterized by a unique trade-off between:
- **Main Lobe Width** (controls transition bandwidth $\Delta f$ of the filter)
- **Side Lobe Level / Peak Side Lobe Attenuation** (controls passband/stopband ripple)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spotlight on a Dark Stage"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you are a theatre director. You have an actor (the *ideal filter response $h_d[n]$*) performing on an *infinitely long, dark stage* (from $n = -\infty$ to $n = +\infty$). A **window function** is your **spotlight**: it illuminates only a small, finite portion of the stage (from $n = 0$ to $n = M$).
> - A **Rectangular window** is a *crude, flat-edged spotlight* — it suddenly flicks ON and OFF. The "pop" of this switching creates ripples (side lobes) in the audience's view (Gibbs phenomenon).
> - A **Hamming window** is a *smoothly tapered spotlight* — it gradually fades IN and OUT, producing a softer transition.
> - A **Blackman window** is an *even smoother, double-tapered spotlight* with two gentle fades — almost imperceptible ripples but at the cost of a wider illuminated patch (wider main lobe → wider transition band).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard FIR Filter Length:** For all three windows, the filter length is $N = M+1$ samples, where $M$ is the **order** of the filter.
> **Universal Boundary Condition:** $w[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $n > M$.

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Filter Order | $M$ | samples |
| Filter Length | $N = M+1$ | samples |
| Main Lobe Width | $\Delta\omega_M$ | radians/sample |
| Peak Side Lobe Attenuation | $A_s$ | dB (decibels) |
| Transition Bandwidth | $\Delta\omega$ | radians/sample |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fixed Windows — Mathematical Forms

### 2.1.1 Rectangular Window

The rectangular window is the simplest window; it corresponds to **abrupt truncation** of the ideal impulse response.

$$w_R[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le n \le M \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Key Properties:**
- It is the *only* window with **no taper**.
- Its frequency response $W_R(e^{j\omega})$ is the **Dirichlet kernel** (a shifted, sampled $\operatorname{sinc}$ function).
- Exhibits the **worst side lobe level** ($\approx \mathbf{-13 \text{ dB}}$) — visible Gibbs oscillations.
- Possesses the **narrowest main lobe** (best frequency resolution, sharpest transition).
- $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\omega(M+1)/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$

---

### 2.1.2 Hamming Window

The Hamming window is a **raised-cosine** window optimized (by R. W. Hamming) to *minimize* the maximum side lobe level.

$$w_H[n] = \begin{cases} 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}\right), & 0 \le n \le M \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Key Properties:**
- Endpoints are *not exactly zero*: $w_H[0] = w_H[M] = 0.08$ (residual ripple).
- Peak side lobe attenuation: $\mathbf{-53 \text{ dB}}$ — vastly better than rectangular.
- Main lobe width: approximately **$\mathbf{8\pi / (M+1)}$** radians/sample (≈ $2\times$ the rectangular main lobe).
- Achieved by **empirical optimization** of the general raised-cosine formula $w[n] = \alpha - (1-\alpha)\cos(2\pi n/M)$ with $\alpha = 0.54$.

---

### 2.1.3 Blackman Window

The Blackman window superimposes a **second cosine harmonic** to further suppress side lobes.

$$w_{Bl}[n] = \begin{cases} 0.42 - 0.50\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}\right) + 0.08\cos\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{M}\right), & 0 \le n \le M \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Key Properties:**
- Two-cosine (double-harmonic) taper → excellent ripple suppression.
- Peak side lobe attenuation: $\mathbf{-74 \text{ dB}}$ — the *highest* of the three fixed windows.
- Main lobe width: approximately **$\mathbf{12\pi / (M+1)}$** radians/sample (≈ $3\times$ the rectangular main lobe).
- The width attenuation trade-off is most extreme for Blackman.

---

## 2.2 The Fundamental Trade-off: "Narrow Main Lobe vs. Low Side Lobes"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept (Examiner's Favourite):**
> As we move from **Rectangular → Hamming → Blackman**, the side lobe level *decreases* (ripple ↓), but the main lobe *widens* (transition band ↑). **You cannot have both a sharp transition AND a smooth passband simultaneously.**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam Quick-Reference)

| Window | Time-Domain Expression $w[n]$, $0\le n\le M$ | Peak Side Lobe Attenuation $A_s$ (dB) | Main Lobe Width $\Delta\omega_M$ (rad/sample) | Transition Band $\Delta\omega$ (rad/sample) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular** | $1$ | $\mathbf{-13}$ dB | $\dfrac{4\pi}{M+1}$ | $\dfrac{0.9\pi}{M}$ |
| **Hamming** | $0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}\right)$ | $\mathbf{-53}$ dB | $\dfrac{8\pi}{M+1}$ | $\dfrac{3.3\pi}{M}$ |
| **Blackman** | $0.42 - 0.50\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}\right) + 0.08\cos\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{M}\right)$ | $\mathbf{-74}$ dB | $\dfrac{12\pi}{M+1}$ | $\dfrac{5.5\pi}{M}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** The transition bandwidth formulas above are **Kaiser empirical approximations** widely used in KTU answer keys. Do NOT confuse with the main lobe width formulas. They differ by a factor of roughly 2.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rectangular**: Used in *spectrum analysis* where frequency resolution is critical and ripple can be tolerated (e.g., coarse DFT leakage studies).
- **Hamming**: The *workhorse* of speech processing, audio equalizers, and biomedical signal filtering (ECG/EEG) — excellent balance of sharpness and smoothness.
- **Blackman**: Used in *precision instrumentation*, radar signal processing, and high-fidelity audio where ripple must be minimized at the cost of a wider transition band.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Frequency Response of the Rectangular Window

### Step 1: Define the window in causal form

$$w_R[n] = u[n] - u[n - (M+1)]$$

This expresses the rectangular window as the difference of two unit-step functions delayed by $M+1$ samples.

### Step 2: Take the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

Recall that $u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi\delta(\omega)$ (in the principal-value sense, for $-\pi \le \omega \le \pi$).

Applying the time-shift property on $u[n-(M+1)]$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} - \dfrac{e^{-j\omega(M+1)}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

### Step 3: Combine and simplify

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-j\omega(M+1)}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

### Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega(M+1)/2}$ and $e^{j\omega/2}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_R(e^{j\omega}) &= \dfrac{1 - e^{-j\omega(M+1)}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} \cdot \dfrac{e^{j\omega(M+1)/2}}{e^{j\omega(M+1)/2}} \cdot \dfrac{e^{j\omega/2}}{e^{j\omega/2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{e^{j\omega(M+1)/2} - e^{-j\omega(M+1)/2}}{e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2j\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\omega(M+1)}{2}\right)}{2j\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= e^{-j\omega M/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\omega(M+1)}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Interpret the result

$$
\boxed{\,W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\omega(M+1)}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)}\,}
$$

This is the **Dirichlet kernel** — a linear-phase, $\operatorname{sinc}$-like function with:
- **Linear phase:** $\angle W_R(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega M/2$ (constant group delay = $M/2$).
- **Magnitude peaks** at $\omega = 0$ with value $M+1$, oscillates with progressively decaying side lobes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How to Choose $M$ (Filter Order) for a Given Specification

For an FIR low-pass filter with:
- Passband edge: $\omega_p$
- Stopband edge: $\omega_s$
- Stopband attenuation: $A_s$ (dB)

The order $M$ is computed from the **transition band** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$:

| Window | Required Attenuation $A_s$ | Empirical $M$ Formula | Numerical Example ($\Delta\omega = 0.1\pi$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $A_s \ge 21$ dB (i.e. $\le 21$ dB stopband spec) | $M \approx \dfrac{0.9\pi}{\Delta\omega}$ | $M \approx 9$ |
| Hamming | $53$ dB $\le A_s$ | $M \approx \dfrac{3.3\pi}{\Delta\omega}$ | $M \approx 33$ |
| Blackman | $74$ dB $\le A_s$ | $M \approx \dfrac{5.5\pi}{\Delta\omega}$ | $M \approx 55$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (commonly tested in KTU):** A higher stopband attenuation requirement forces a *larger* filter order $M$, which in turn widens the *main lobe* but *suppresses* side lobes.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Hamming Window Coefficients

**Problem:** Design a length-$N = 7$ ($M = 6$) FIR low-pass filter using a Hamming window, for an ideal cut-off $\omega_c = \pi/2$.

**Step A:** Compute the ideal (sinc) impulse response $h_d[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_d[n] &= \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\omega_c \left(n - \dfrac{M}{2}\right)\right)}{\pi \left(n - \dfrac{M}{2}\right)} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}(n - 3)\right)}{\pi (n - 3)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B:** Evaluate $h_d[n]$ at $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 6$:

| $n$ | $n - 3$ | $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}(n-3)\right)$ | $h_d[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-3$ | $+1$ | $-1/(3\pi) = -0.1061$ |
| 1 | $-2$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 2 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $+1/\pi = +0.3183$ |
| 3 | $0$ | $0$ | $0.5$ (by L'Hôpital) |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $+1/\pi = +0.3183$ |
| 5 | $+2$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 6 | $+3$ | $-1$ | $-1/(3\pi) = -0.1061$ |

**Step C:** Compute the Hamming window coefficients $w_H[n]$:

$$
w_H[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{6}\right) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{3}\right)
$$

| $n$ | $\cos(\pi n / 3)$ | $w_H[n]$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $+1$ | $0.08$ |
| 1 | $+0.5$ | $0.31$ |
| 2 | $-0.5$ | $0.77$ |
| 3 | $-1$ | $1.00$ |
| 4 | $-0.5$ | $0.77$ |
| 5 | $+0.5$ | $0.31$ |
| 6 | $+1$ | $0.08$ |

**Step D:** Multiply $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w_H[n]$:

| $n$ | $h_d[n]$ | $w_H[n]$ | $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w_H[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-0.1061$ | $0.08$ | $-0.0085$ |
| 1 | $0$ | $0.31$ | $0$ |
| 2 | $0.3183$ | $0.77$ | $0.2451$ |
| 3 | $0.5$ | $1.00$ | $0.5$ |
| 4 | $0.3183$ | $0.77$ | $0.2451$ |
| 5 | $0$ | $0.31$ | $0$ |
| 6 | $-0.1061$ | $0.08$ | $-0.0085$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Observe the **even symmetry** of $h[n]$: $h[0] = h[6]$, $h[1] = h[5]$, $h[2] = h[4]$. This guarantees **linear phase** (Type-I FIR).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Window-Method FIR Filter Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Specify Filter Requirements] --> B[Step 2: Derive Ideal Impulse Response hd n]
    B --> C{Step 3: Choose Window Type}
    C --> D1[Rectangular: M = 0.9pi / dW]
    C --> D2[Hamming: M = 3.3pi / dW]
    C --> D3[Blackman: M = 5.5pi / dW]
    D1 --> E[Step 4: Generate Window Coeffs w n]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Step 5: Multiply h n = hd n times w n]
    F --> G[Step 6: Verify Linear Phase and Attenuation]
    G --> H[Realizable FIR Filter h n]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The pipeline takes digital filter specifications, derives the ideal (infinite) impulse response, selects a window based on the required stopband attenuation, generates the window coefficients, pointwise-multiplies them with the ideal response, and yields the final causal FIR filter.

---

## 4.2 Architecture Flow: Time vs Frequency Domain Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Domain Window w n] --> B[DTFT Operation]
    B --> C[Frequency Response W ej omega]
    C --> D{Main Lobe Width}
    C --> E{Side Lobe Level}
    D --> F[Sharpness of Transition Band]
    E --> G[Passband and Stopband Ripple]
    F --> H{Inverse Trade off Relation}
    G --> H
    H --> I[Narrow Main Lobe gives Large Side Lobes]
    H --> J[Wide Main Lobe gives Small Side Lobes]
    I --> K[Rectangular Window]
    J --> L[Blackman Window]
```

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Three Windows Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RECT[Rectangular Window]
        R1[w n = 1 for 0 le n le M]
        R2[Main Lobe: Narrow 4pi over N]
        R3[Side Lobes: High minus 13 dB]
        R1 --> R2
        R2 --> R3
    end
    subgraph HAMM[Hamming Window]
        H1[w n = 0.54 minus 0.46 cos 2pin over M]
        H2[Main Lobe: 2x Rectangular]
        H3[Side Lobes: minus 53 dB]
        H1 --> H2
        H2 --> H3
    end
    subgraph BLAC[Blackman Window]
        B1[w n has TWO cosine harmonics]
        B2[Main Lobe: 3x Rectangular]
        B3[Side Lobes: minus 74 dB]
        B1 --> B2
        B2 --> B3
    end
    RECT --> TRADE[Main Lobe vs Side Lobe Trade off]
    HAMM --> TRADE
    BLAC --> TRADE
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Data-Flow: Multiplication of Ideal Response with Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[hd n Ideal Infinite Response] --> MUL[Pointwise Multiplication Operator]
    B[w n Finite Window] --> MUL
    MUL --> C[h n Realizable Causal FIR Filter]
    C --> D[DTFT to Get H ej omega]
    D --> E[Verify Spec Passband and Stopband]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

### Q1. Compare the main lobe width and side lobe level of Rectangular, Hamming, and Blackman windows.

**Model Answer:**

The three fixed windows exhibit a fundamental trade-off between main lobe width and side lobe attenuation:

| Window | Main Lobe Width (rad/sample) | Peak Side Lobe Attenuation |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $\dfrac{4\pi}{M+1}$ (narrowest) | $-13$ dB (worst) |
| Hamming | $\dfrac{8\pi}{M+1}$ | $-53$ dB |
| Blackman | $\dfrac{12\pi}{M+1}$ (widest) | $-74$ dB (best) |

> The Rectangular window has the *narrowest* main lobe (best selectivity) but *largest* side lobe ripples (worst stopband behaviour). The Blackman window reverses this: *widest* main lobe (worst selectivity) but *best* ripple suppression. Hamming is the *balanced* compromise.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

### Q2. What is the role of a window function in FIR filter design? Why is a Rectangular window rarely preferred in practice?

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the Window Function:**
A window function $w[n]$ is a finite-length, even-symmetric sequence that is pointwise-multiplied with the *ideal* infinite impulse response $h_d[n]$ of a target filter. This multiplication:
- Truncates the ideal response to a finite length $N = M+1$, making the filter **realizable** (causal and physically implementable).
- Preserves the **linear phase** property (since $w[n]$ is even-symmetric).
- Shapes the **frequency-domain response**, particularly the trade-off between main lobe width and side lobe level.

**Why Rectangular is Rarely Preferred:**
The rectangular window ($w[n] = 1$) is equivalent to *abrupt truncation* of $h_d[n]$. Its frequency response is the Dirichlet kernel, which exhibits the **Gibbs phenomenon** — the maximum side lobe level is only $\mathbf{-13 \text{ dB}}$. This causes:
- Excessive **passband and stopband ripples**, distorting the desired frequency response.
- Severe **leakage** in spectral analysis applications.
- A peak overshoot of ~9% near the passband edge that does *not* decrease with increasing filter length $M$.

In contrast, tapered windows (Hamming, Blackman, etc.) trade main lobe width for *much* lower side lobes, yielding smoother passband/stopband behaviour.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

---

### 🔹 Question A: (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the time-domain expression for the **Hamming window** and justify the choice of the coefficients $0.54$ and $0.46$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** For a low-pass FIR filter design using a Hamming window with filter length $N = 51$ and cut-off frequency $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ radians/sample, determine the filter coefficients $h[n]$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Derivation of the Hamming Window:**

The Hamming window is a special case of the *generalized raised-cosine* (Hann) window family:

$$w[n] = \alpha - (1 - \alpha) \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}\right), \quad 0 \le n \le M$$

**Step 1 — Why a raised cosine?**
A pure Hann window ($\alpha = 0.5$) has its first side lobe at $-31$ dB. Hamming *empirically searched* for the value of $\alpha$ that **minimizes the peak side lobe level** (specifically, the first side lobe).

**Step 2 — Optimization Condition:**
To cancel the first side lobe of the Dirichlet kernel, the D.C. and first-harmonic components of the window's spectrum must satisfy a specific ratio. The optimal weighting is:

$$\alpha = 0.54, \quad (1 - \alpha) = 0.46$$

**Step 3 — Resulting Window:**

$$\boxed{\,w_H[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}\right), \quad 0 \le n \le M\,}$$

**Justification of Coefficients:**
- The constant term $\alpha = 0.54$ corresponds to the D.C. (zero-frequency) component of the window spectrum.
- The coefficient $0.46$ weights the first cosine harmonic.
- Together, they are tuned so that the *first* side lobe of $W_H(e^{j\omega})$ is forced close to zero, giving a peak side lobe attenuation of $\mathbf{-53 \text{ dB}}$.
- The endpoints: $w_H[0] = w_H[M] = 0.54 - 0.46 = 0.08$ (not exactly zero — a defining feature of the Hamming vs. the Hann window).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $N = 51$ (so $M = N - 1 = 50$); cut-off $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample.

**Step 1 — Ideal impulse response:**

$$h_d[n] = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\omega_c \left(n - \dfrac{M}{2}\right)\right)}{\pi\left(n - \dfrac{M}{2}\right)} = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(0.4\pi(n - 25)\right)}{\pi(n - 25)}, \quad 0 \le n \le 50$$

**Step 2 — Hamming window coefficients:**

$$w_H[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{50}\right) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{25}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Sample evaluations (key points):**

| $n$ | $h_d[n]$ | $w_H[n]$ | $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w_H[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\dfrac{\sin(-10\pi)}{-25\pi} = 0$ | $0.08$ | $0$ |
| 25 | $0.4$ (from L'Hôpital) | $1.00$ | $0.4000$ |
| 50 | $0$ | $0.08$ | $0$ |

**Step 4 — General formula for the answer:**

$$\boxed{\,h[n] = \left[\dfrac{\sin\!\left(0.4\pi(n - 25)\right)}{\pi(n - 25)}\right] \cdot \left[0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{25}\right)\right], \quad 0 \le n \le 50\,}$$

**Verification:** $h[n]$ is **even-symmetric** about $n = 25$, confirming **Type-I linear-phase** FIR structure. Peak side lobe attenuation of the resulting filter: $\approx -53$ dB.

---

### 🔹 Question B: (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the *frequency-domain trade-off* between main lobe width and side lobe attenuation among Rectangular, Hamming, and Blackman windows. Use a suitable table and discuss which window is most suitable for a stopband attenuation of 60 dB. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Design a low-pass FIR filter of order $M = 20$ using a **Blackman window** with cut-off frequency $\omega_c = 0.3\pi$ rad/sample. State the **first 5 filter coefficients** $h[0]$ through $h[4]$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**The Fundamental Trade-off:**
In window-based FIR design, the window choice determines the spectral leakage pattern of the filter. There exists a *universal inverse relationship* between two metrics:
- **Main Lobe Width** $\Delta\omega_M$ (controls filter transition sharpness)
- **Peak Side Lobe Attenuation** $A_s$ (controls passband/stopband ripple)

> As $\Delta\omega_M$ *decreases* (sharper transition), $A_s$ *increases* (worse ripple), and vice versa.

**Comparative Table:**

| Window | Main Lobe Width (rad/sample) | Side Lobe Attenuation $A_s$ | Passband/Stopband Ripple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $\dfrac{4\pi}{M+1}$ | $-13$ dB | ~9% overshoot (Gibbs) |
| Hamming | $\dfrac{8\pi}{M+1}$ | $-53$ dB | Very small |
| Blackman | $\dfrac{12\pi}{M+1}$ | $-74$ dB | Negligible |

**Choice for 60 dB Stopband Attenuation:**
- Rectangular ($\mathbf{-13 \text{ dB}}$) — **inadequate**, fails the 60 dB spec.
- Hamming ($\mathbf{-53 \text{ dB}}$) — **inadequate**, just below 60 dB.
- **Blackman ($\mathbf{-74 \text{ dB}}$) — SUITABLE**, as $-74$ dB $\le -60$ dB (provides 14 dB margin).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The **Blackman window** is the appropriate choice. The price to pay is a *wider transition band* (≈ $3\times$ the rectangular's), which must be compensated by increasing the filter order $M$ if the transition width is critical.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** Filter order $M = 20$ (so $N = 21$, midpoint = $M/2 = 10$); cut-off $\omega_c = 0.3\pi$ rad/sample.

**Step 1 — Ideal impulse response:**

$$h_d[n] = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(0.3\pi(n - 10)\right)}{\pi(n - 10)}, \quad 0 \le n \le 20$$

**Step 2 — Blackman window coefficients:**

$$w_{Bl}[n] = 0.42 - 0.50\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{20}\right) + 0.08\cos\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{20}\right) = 0.42 - 0.50\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{10}\right) + 0.08\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n}{5}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $h_d[n]$ and $w_{Bl}[n]$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$:**

| $n$ | $n - 10$ | $h_d[n]$ | $\cos(\pi n/10)$ | $\cos(\pi n/5)$ | $w_{Bl}[n]$ | $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w_{Bl}[n]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-10$ | $\dfrac{\sin(-3\pi)}{-10\pi} = 0$ | $+1$ | $+1$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 1 | $-9$ | $\dfrac{\sin(-2.7\pi)}{-9\pi} = \dfrac{0.4122}{9\pi} = 0.0146$ | $0.9511$ | $0.8090$ | $0.42 - 0.4755 + 0.0647 = 0.0092$ | $\approx 0.000134$ |
| 2 | $-8$ | $\dfrac{\sin(-2.4\pi)}{-8\pi} = \dfrac{0.8090}{8\pi} = 0.0322$ | $0.8090$ | $0.3090$ | $0.42 - 0.4045 + 0.0247 = 0.0402$ | $\approx 0.001295$ |
| 3 | $-7$ | $\dfrac{\sin(-2.1\pi)}{-7\pi} = \dfrac{0.8090}{7\pi} = 0.0368$ | $0.5878$ | $-0.3090$ | $0.42 - 0.2939 - 0.0247 = 0.1014$ | $\approx 0.003732$ |
| 4 | $-6$ | $\dfrac{\sin(-1.8\pi)}{-6\pi} = \dfrac{-0.4122}{-6\pi} = 0.0219$ | $0.3090$ | $-0.8090$ | $0.42 - 0.1545 - 0.0647 = 0.2008$ | $\approx 0.004395$ |

**Final Answer (First 5 Coefficients):**

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
h[0] &= 0 \\
h[1] &\approx 1.34 \times 10^{-4} \\
h[2] &\approx 1.30 \times 10^{-3} \\
h[3] &\approx 3.73 \times 10^{-3} \\
h[4] &\approx 4.40 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the Blackman window values are *very small* at the edges (exactly 0 at $n=0$ and $n=20$), which is the **defining feature** distinguishing it from the Hamming window (which is only $0.08$ at the edges).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the cosine argument:** The correct argument is $\dfrac{2\pi n}{M}$, NOT $\dfrac{2\pi n}{N}$. Using $N$ instead of $M$ is a *frequently penalized* mistake.
> 2. **Hamming endpoints not zero:** Many students assume Hamming window endpoints are zero (like Hann). They are **0.08**, not 0. This is *exam-board critical*.
> 3. **Forgetting the L'Hôpital step** at $n = M/2$ for $h_d[n]$: the value is $\omega_c/\pi$, not undefined.
> 4. **Sign errors in $h_d[n]$:** The numerator is $\sin(\omega_c(n - M/2))$, not $\sin(\omega_c(n + M/2))$. Be careful with the *causal* shift.
> 5. **Main lobe vs. transition band:** Don't write the main lobe formula in a question asking for transition bandwidth — they are related but different quantities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Summary — Memorize for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ A **window** truncates the ideal infinite impulse response to a finite-length causal FIR filter.
- ✅ The **Rectangular window** is the abrupt truncation window: $w_R[n] = 1$, side lobes $\mathbf{-13 \text{ dB}}$.
- ✅ The **Hamming window** is a raised-cosine window: $w_H[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/M)$, side lobes $\mathbf{-53 \text{ dB}}$.
- ✅ The **Blackman window** is a double-cosine window: $w_{Bl}[n] = 0.42 - 0.5\cos(2\pi n/M) + 0.08\cos(4\pi n/M)$, side lobes $\mathbf{-74 \text{ dB}}$.
- ✅ Main lobe widths: Rectangular $\dfrac{4\pi}{M+1}$, Hamming $\dfrac{8\pi}{M+1}$, Blackman $\dfrac{12\pi}{M+1}$.
- ✅ **Trade-off rule:** Smaller side lobes ⇔ Wider main lobe ⇔ Wider transition band.
- ✅ **Filter length:** $N = M + 1$. All windows are defined for $0 \le n \le M$.
- ✅ **Linear phase** is preserved *only* when the window is **even-symmetric** about $n = M/2$ — true for all three fixed windows.
- ✅ **Hamming endpoint value:** $0.08$ (NOT zero).
- ✅ **Blackman endpoint value:** exactly $0$.
- ✅ For a **60 dB stopband spec**, choose **Blackman** (provides 14 dB margin).
- ✅ For a **53 dB stopband spec**, **Hamming** is sufficient and gives a *narrower* transition band.
- ✅ The frequency response of the rectangular window is the **Dirichlet kernel**: $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\omega(M+1)/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$.
- ✅ Empirical filter order formulas (Kaiser): $M \approx 0.9\pi/\Delta\omega$ (Rect), $3.3\pi/\Delta\omega$ (Hamming), $5.5\pi/\Delta\omega$ (Blackman).
- ✅ The **Gibbs phenomenon** (~9% overshoot) is *inherent* to the rectangular window and cannot be removed by increasing $M$.
- ✅ KTU exam traps: Watch the cosine argument ($2\pi n / M$ vs. $2\pi n / N$); always apply L'Hôpital at the centre tap; preserve even symmetry in $h[n]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adjustable Window Function: Kaiser Window, Problem Solving Using Software Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT601 - DISCRETE TIME SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adjustable Window Function: Kaiser Window, Problem Solving Using Software Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Kaiser Window** is an *adjustable* (parametric) window function widely used in the design of Finite Impulse Response (FIR) digital filters. It is defined as a discrete-time sequence $w[n]$ of length $N+1$ samples, where each sample is computed from the **modified Bessel function of the first kind, zeroth order, denoted $I_0(\cdot)$**.

For a length $N+1$ window (i.e., $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$), the Kaiser window is mathematically expressed as:

$$
w[n] = \frac{I_0\!\left[\beta \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{n - N/2}{N/2}\right)^{\!2}}\,\right]}{I_0(\beta)}, \quad 0 \le n \le N
$$

$$
w[n] = 0, \quad \text{otherwise}
$$

The parameter $\beta$ (the *shape parameter*) is the **tuning knob** of the Kaiser window. By varying $\beta$, the designer can continuously trade off **main lobe width** (which determines transition bandwidth) against **side lobe attenuation** (which determines stopband ripple). This is the reason it is termed the *most flexible* window in DSP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Adjustable vs. Fixed Windows:**
> Earlier windows (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) have *fixed* trade-offs. The Kaiser window is the *only* window that lets the designer **explicitly specify the stopband attenuation in dB**, and the window parameters (both $\beta$ and filter order $N$) are then *automatically* chosen to meet the specification. This is why it is the **recommended default** in KTU-lab standard FIR design software (MATLAB's `fir1` and `kaiserord` functions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Modified Bessel Function of the First Kind (Order 0):**
> The function $I_0(x)$ is defined by the infinite power series:
> $$I_0(x) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left[\frac{(x/2)^k}{k!}\right]^{\!2} = 1 + \frac{x^2}{4} + \frac{x^4}{64} + \frac{x^6}{2304} + \cdots$$
> It is a positive, monotonically increasing, even function of $x$. In practice, only 15–25 terms of this series are needed to achieve double-precision accuracy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tuning a **musical equaliser** with five sliders. The first four sliders (Rectangular, Hanning, Hamming, Blackman) are *fixed presets* — you cannot move them. The **fifth slider is the Kaiser window**, and it is *continuously variable* between 0% and 100%.

- **$\beta = 0$** (slider at 0%): the window degenerates into a **Rectangular window** — the response is sharp, but you hear a lot of "ringing" (high side lobes, poor stopband attenuation).
- **$\beta$ large** (slider at 100%): the window becomes very smooth — ringing nearly vanishes (low side lobes, excellent stopband attenuation), but the response becomes "mushy" (wide main lobe, wide transition band).

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Intuition — Why does $\beta$ do this?**
> $I_0$ is *convex* (curves upward). When $\beta$ is small, the ratio inside the square root is close to 1 for most $n$, so the denominator $I_0(\beta)$ nearly equals the numerator, and $w[n] \approx 1$ (rectangular). When $\beta$ is large, the numerator is much *larger* in the centre than at the edges, so the window *tapers* aggressively to zero at the ends, smoothing the truncation ripple.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Window length | $N+1$ | 5 – 200 | samples |
| Shape parameter | $\beta$ | 0 – 10 (commonly 0 – 8) | dimensionless |
| Stopband attenuation | $A$ | 21 – 100 | **dB** |
| Transition width | $\Delta \omega$ | $0.01\pi$ – $0.3\pi$ | rad/sample |
| Bessel function order | 0 | fixed | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant $\mathbf{2.285}$ and $\mathbf{8}$ appearing later in the empirical formulas for $\beta$ and $N$ are *empirically fitted* constants derived by James Kaiser (1974) after extensive numerical optimisation across thousands of filter specifications. They are **not** derived from first principles — they are curve-fit numbers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Kaiser Formula

The expression $w[n]$ can be split into three functional pieces that the student must recognise:

1. **The Index Normalisation** — $\dfrac{n - N/2}{N/2}$ is a *symmetric* term that runs from $-1$ (at $n=0$) through $0$ (at $n=N/2$) to $+1$ (at $n=N$). It guarantees that $w[n]$ is symmetric (even) about the centre sample.
2. **The Radial Profile** — $\sqrt{1 - (\cdot)^2}$ traces a *unit semi-circle*. This is the key shape: it forces $w[n]$ to be **maximum (= 1) at the centre** and **decreases monotonically** toward the ends.
3. **The Bessel Ratio** — dividing by $I_0(\beta)$ is a **normalisation** step so that the peak value of the window is exactly $1$ (occurs at $n = N/2$, where the argument of $I_0$ is $\beta$, hence numerator = denominator).

### 2.2 Why Does the Kaiser Window Exist? — The "Why" Behind It

The Kaiser window is the **time-domain window corresponding to the Discrete Prolate Spheroidal Sequences (DPSS)**, which are the sequences that *maximise* the energy concentration in a given frequency band. Among all windows of length $N+1$, the DPSS has the **smallest main-lobe energy leakage for a given side-lobe level**. Because the DPSS cannot be written in closed form, Kaiser found a **simple, near-optimal approximation** using the Bessel function — hence the name "Kaiser window".

### 2.3 The Design Equations (Kaiser's Empirical Formulas)

To design an FIR filter using the Kaiser window, the engineer starts with **four specifications**:
- Passband edge: $\omega_p$
- Stopband edge: $\omega_s$ (with $\omega_s > \omega_p$)
- Passband ripple: $\delta_p$
- Stopband ripple: $\delta_s$

From these, three derived quantities are computed:

**Step 1 — Effective Stopband Attenuation $A$ (in dB):**

$$
A = -20 \log_{10}\!\left[\min(\delta_p,\, \delta_s)\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students frequently lose marks here. $A$ is always **rounded up to the next integer** and is **positive** (because $\delta < 1$ implies $20\log\delta < 0$, so the minus sign makes $A > 0$). For example, $\delta_s = 0.01 \Rightarrow A = 40$ **dB**.

**Step 2 — Shape Parameter $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \begin{cases}
0.1102\,(A - 8.7), & A > 50 \\
0.5842\,(A - 21)^{0.4} + 0.07886\,(A - 21), & 21 \le A \le 50 \\
0, & A < 21
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation of $\beta$:**
> - $\beta = 0 \Rightarrow$ Rectangular window (used only for very tolerant designs, $A < 21$ dB).
> - $\beta \approx 4$ to $6 \Rightarrow$ Hamming-like behaviour ($A \approx 50$ dB).
> - $\beta \approx 8$ to $10 \Rightarrow$ Blackman-like behaviour ($A \approx 75$ to 100 dB).

**Step 3 — Filter Order $N$ (window length $= N+1$):**

$$
N = \left\lceil\,\frac{A - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega}\,\right\rceil, \quad \text{where } \Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p
$$

The ceiling $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures $N$ is the **smallest integer** that meets the spec.

**Step 4 — Impulse Response of the Filter:**

$$
h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad 0 \le n \le N
$$

where $h_d[n]$ is the **ideal (infinite-length) impulse response** of the desired filter (lowpass, highpass, bandpass, or bandstop), and $w[n]$ is the Kaiser window computed with the $\beta$ and $N$ from the previous steps.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kaiser window sample | $w[n] = I_0\!\left[\beta\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{n-N/2}{N/2}\right)^{\!2}}\right] / I_0(\beta)$ | $0 \le n \le N$, else 0 |
| 2 | Modified Bessel $I_0(x)$ | $1 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\left[\dfrac{(x/2)^k}{k!}\right]^{\!2}$ | Converges for all $x \ge 0$ |
| 3 | Stopband attenuation (dB) | $A = -20\log_{10}\!\left[\min(\delta_p,\delta_s)\right]$ | Round up to next integer |
| 4 | Shape parameter $\beta$ (high) | $0.1102(A - 8.7)$ | $A > 50$ |
| 5 | Shape parameter $\beta$ (mid) | $0.5842(A-21)^{0.4} + 0.07886(A-21)$ | $21 \le A \le 50$ |
| 6 | Shape parameter $\beta$ (low) | $0$ | $A < 21$ |
| 7 | Transition width | $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ | rad/sample |
| 8 | Filter order | $N = \lceil (A-8)/(2.285\,\Delta\omega) \rceil$ | $N$ = smallest integer |
| 9 | Window length | $L = N + 1$ | samples |
| 10 | Filter output equation | $h[n] = h_d[n]\cdot w[n]$ | Truncation multiplication |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $A$ (attenuation in dB, **positive** number) with the filter *amplitude* response. Students routinely substitute $-A$ or use natural log instead of $\log_{10}$ — both are wrong and cost 1 mark each.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Kaiser window is the **industry default** in professional FIR filter design. It is used in:

- **Audio codecs (MP3, AAC, Opus)** for the analysis/synthesis filter banks.
- **Digital communication receivers** (matched filters, pulse-shaping filters).
- **Biomedical signal processing** (ECG/EEG bandpass filters where the stopband attenuation must exceed 60 dB to suppress powerline noise).
- **Radar and sonar** for matched filtering of chirp signals.
- **MATLAB's `fir1(..., kaiser(N,Wn,beta))` and Python's `scipy.signal.firwin(..., window='kaiser')`** both invoke the Kaiser window directly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Normalisation Property $w[N/2] = 1$

The student must verify that the **central sample** of the Kaiser window is always equal to $1$ (regardless of $\beta$), confirming the window is *normalised to unity peak*.

**Starting from the definition:**

$$
w\!\left[\tfrac{N}{2}\right] = \frac{I_0\!\left[\beta \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{N/2 - N/2}{N/2}\right)^{\!2}}\,\right]}{I_0(\beta)}
$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the inner index term:**

$$
\frac{N/2 - N/2}{N/2} = \frac{0}{N/2} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back into the square root:**

$$
\sqrt{1 - 0^2} = \sqrt{1} = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Bessel function argument:**

$$
I_0\!\left[\beta \cdot 1\right] = I_0(\beta)
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**

$$
w\!\left[\tfrac{N}{2}\right] = \frac{I_0(\beta)}{I_0(\beta)} = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** The central sample of the Kaiser window is **identically equal to 1**, confirming DC-normalisation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Boundary Values $w[0] = w[N] = 1/I_0(\beta)$

**Starting from the definition at $n=0$:**

$$
w[0] = \frac{I_0\!\left[\beta \sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{0 - N/2}{N/2}\right)^{\!2}}\,\right]}{I_0(\beta)}
$$

**Step 1 — Inner index term at $n=0$:**

$$
\frac{0 - N/2}{N/2} = \frac{-N/2}{N/2} = -1
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and square:**

$$
(-1)^2 = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Square root:**

$$
\sqrt{1 - 1} = \sqrt{0} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Bessel function argument:**

$$
I_0\!\left[\beta \cdot 0\right] = I_0(0) = 1
$$

(Recall from the power series: $I_0(0) = 1$.)

**Step 5 — Form the ratio:**

$$
w[0] = \frac{1}{I_0(\beta)}
$$

By symmetry ($w[n] = w[N-n]$), the same holds at $n = N$:

$$
w[N] = \frac{1}{I_0(\beta)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **$\beta$ controls the *taper depth* at the edges**: the larger $\beta$ is, the larger $I_0(\beta)$, and hence the *smaller* the edge value. For $\beta = 5$, $I_0(5) \approx 27.24$, so $w[0] \approx 0.0367$. For $\beta = 8$, $I_0(8) \approx 427.6$, so $w[0] \approx 0.00234$ — a much stronger taper.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Designing a Lowpass FIR Filter

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** Design a lowpass FIR filter using a Kaiser window with the following specifications:
> - Passband edge: $\omega_p = 0.3\pi$ rad/sample
> - Stopband edge: $\omega_s = 0.5\pi$ rad/sample
> - Passband ripple: $\delta_p = 0.01$
> - Stopband ripple: $\delta_s = 0.001$

**Step 1 — Compute the required stopband attenuation $A$:**

$$
A = -20 \log_{10}\!\left[\min(\delta_p,\, \delta_s)\right] = -20 \log_{10}\!\left[\min(0.01,\, 0.001)\right]
$$

The minimum of $\{0.01, 0.001\}$ is $0.001$, hence:

$$
A = -20 \log_{10}(0.001) = -20 \times (-3) = 60 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the shape parameter $\beta$:**

Since $A = 60 > 50$, we use the *high-attenuation* formula:

$$
\beta = 0.1102 \times (A - 8.7) = 0.1102 \times (60 - 8.7)
$$

$$
\beta = 0.1102 \times 51.3 = 5.654
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the transition width $\Delta\omega$:**

$$
\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p = 0.5\pi - 0.3\pi = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the filter order $N$:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{A - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{60 - 8}{2.285 \times 0.2\pi} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4a — Numerator:** $60 - 8 = 52$

**Step 4b — Denominator:** $2.285 \times 0.2\pi = 2.285 \times 0.6283 = 1.4357$

**Step 4c — Ratio:** $52 / 1.4357 = 36.22$

**Step 4d — Apply ceiling:** $N = \lceil 36.22 \rceil = 37$

Hence the filter is of order 37, with window length $L = N + 1 = 38$ samples.

**Step 5 — Compute the Kaiser window samples for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, 37$:**

We compute the **index term** for $n = 0$ as a sanity check:

$$
\frac{n - N/2}{N/2} = \frac{0 - 18.5}{18.5} = -1
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 - (-1)^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w[0] = \frac{I_0(0)}{I_0(5.654)} = \frac{1}{287.81} \approx 0.003474
$$

For $n = 18$ (near centre):

$$
\frac{18 - 18.5}{18.5} = -0.0270
$$

$$
\sqrt{1 - 0.0270^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.000730} = \sqrt{0.99927} = 0.99963
$$

$$
w[18] = \frac{I_0(5.654 \times 0.99963)}{I_0(5.654)} = \frac{I_0(5.652)}{I_0(5.654)} \approx 0.9999
$$

For $n = 19$ (true centre, $n = N/2 = 18.5$, but with integer index, the closest are 18 and 19 — the window is symmetric).

**Step 6 — Compute the ideal lowpass impulse response $h_d[n]$:**

For a lowpass filter with cutoff $\omega_c = (\omega_p + \omega_s)/2 = 0.4\pi$:

$$
h_d[n] = \begin{cases}
\dfrac{\sin(\omega_c (n - N/2))}{\pi (n - N/2)}, & n \ne N/2 \\
\dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = N/2
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 7 — Final filter impulse response:**

$$
h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad 0 \le n \le 37
$$

This 38-tap FIR filter will meet (or exceed) the prescribed $A = 60$ dB stopband attenuation with transition width $\Delta\omega = 0.2\pi$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of $\beta$ on Window Properties

| $\beta$ value | Equivalent Window | Approx. $A$ (dB) | Main Lobe Width (bins) | Side Lobe Level (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | Rectangular | 13 (theoretical) | 2 | $-13$ |
| 1.5 | — | ~25 | 2.4 | $-25$ |
| 3.0 | — | ~37 | 2.8 | $-37$ |
| 4.55 | Hamming-equivalent | ~50 | 3.3 | $-50$ |
| 5.65 | — | ~60 | 3.7 | $-60$ |
| 8.0 | Blackman-equivalent | ~74 | 4.6 | $-74$ |
| 10.0 | — | ~92 | 5.4 | $-92$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the clear monotonic trend: **larger $\beta$ $\Rightarrow$ lower side-lobe level** (good for stopband) **but wider main lobe** (bad for transition width). This is the fundamental trade-off that the Kaiser window *exposes as a single tuning parameter*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — FIR Filter Design Workflow using Kaiser Window

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Filter Specs omega_p, omega_s, delta_p, delta_s] --> B[Compute A = -20 log10 min delta_p, delta_s]
    B --> C{A greater than 50?}
    C -- Yes --> D[beta = 0.1102 times A minus 8.7]
    C -- No --> E{A between 21 and 50?}
    E -- Yes --> F[beta = 0.5842 times A minus 21 to 0.4 plus 0.07886 times A minus 21]
    E -- No --> G[beta = 0]
    D --> H[Compute Delta omega = omega_s minus omega_p]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute N = ceil of A minus 8 over 2.285 times Delta omega]
    I --> J[Generate Kaiser window w of n with beta and N]
    J --> K[Compute ideal impulse response h_d of n for desired filter type]
    K --> L[Multiply: h of n = h_d of n times w of n]
    L --> M[Output: FIR filter coefficients h of n of length N plus 1]
    M --> N[Validate: Check actual A and Delta omega meet specs]
```

### 4.2 State Diagram — $\beta$ Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compute A in dB] --> B{A less than 21}
    B -- Yes --> C[beta = 0 Rectangular]
    B -- No --> D{A in 21 to 50}
    D -- Yes --> E[beta = 0.5842 A minus 21 to 0.4 plus 0.07886 A minus 21]
    D -- No --> F{A greater than 50}
    F -- Yes --> G[beta = 0.1102 A minus 8.7]
    C --> H[Ready: Generate Kaiser window]
    E --> H
    G --> H
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Diagram — Windowing Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ID[n] --> MP[/Multiplication point/]
    KW[Kaiser window w of n] --> MP
    MP --> OUT[Truncated, smoothed h of n]
    note1[Ideal: infinite-length sharp response] -.-> ID
    note2[Bessel-shaped taper] -.-> KW
    note3[Truncated, smooth impulse response] -.-> OUT
```

### 4.4 Frequency-Response Trade-off Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LO["Low beta regime (beta = 0 to 3)"]
        L1[Sharp transition] --> L2[Poor stopband attenuation A less than 37 dB]
        L2 --> L3[High side lobes]
    end
    subgraph MD["Mid beta regime (beta = 3 to 6)"]
        M1[Moderate transition] --> M2[Good stopband attenuation 37 to 65 dB]
        M2 --> M3[Moderate side lobes]
    end
    subgraph HI["High beta regime (beta = 6 to 10)"]
        H1[Wide transition] --> H2[Excellent stopband attenuation A greater than 65 dB]
        H2 --> H3[Negligible side lobes]
    end
    LO --> MD
    MD --> HI
```

### 4.5 Software-Tool Problem-Solving Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MATLAB Python or Scilab] --> B[Step 1: Define specifications]
    B --> C[Step 2: Call kaiserord to compute N and beta]
    C --> D[Step 3: Call fir1 with kaiser window]
    D --> E[Step 4: freqz to plot magnitude and phase]
    E --> F[Step 5: Verify spec compliance]
    F --> G{Specs met?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Output validated filter]
    G -- No --> I[Increase N or beta and re iterate]
    I --> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **Tool Mapping (for KTU Lab Viva):**
> - **MATLAB:** `kaiserord(freqs, mags, devs, Fs)` returns `[N, Wn, beta, ftype]`. Then `b = fir1(N, Wn, ftype, kaiser(N+1, beta))`. Plot with `fvtool(b)`.
> - **Python (SciPy):** `numtaps, beta = signal.kaiserord(ripple, width)` then `taps = signal.firwin(numtaps, cutoff, window=('kaiser', beta))`. Plot with `scipy.signal.freqz`.
> - **Scilab:** `w = kaiser(N+1, beta)`, then `hn = w .* hd` where `hd` is the ideal response computed manually.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is an adjustable window? Why is the Kaiser window classified as one? Mention the role of the parameter $\beta$.*

**Model Answer:**

An **adjustable window** is a window function whose spectral characteristics (main-lobe width and side-lobe level) can be **continuously varied** by changing one or more *shape parameters*, rather than being fixed as in classical windows (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman).

The **Kaiser window** is classified as adjustable because its frequency-domain trade-off is controlled by a **single real-valued parameter $\beta$**:

- $\beta = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ window degenerates to the **rectangular** window (narrowest main lobe, highest side lobes).
- $\beta$ large $\Rightarrow$ window becomes smooth, **side lobes drop, main lobe widens**.

The parameter $\beta$ is determined from the desired stopband attenuation $A$ using the empirical Kaiser formulas. Thus, for a given filter specification, the designer obtains a *tailor-made* window — the essence of "adjustability". (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the mathematical expression for the Kaiser window of length $N+1$ and explain the role of the modified Bessel function $I_0(x)$ in it.*

**Model Answer:**

The Kaiser window of length $N+1$ is given by:

$$
w[n] = \frac{I_0\!\left[\beta\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{n-N/2}{N/2}\right)^{\!2}}\right]}{I_0(\beta)}, \quad 0 \le n \le N
$$

The **modified Bessel function of the first kind, order zero**, $I_0(x)$:

- Is a **positive, even, monotonically increasing** function of $x$ for $x \ge 0$, with $I_0(0) = 1$.
- Provides a **smooth, convex taper** that is *superior* to cosine-based tapers in minimising spectral leakage.
- Ensures the window has a **near-DPSS (Discrete Prolate Spheroidal Sequence) frequency concentration**, which is mathematically optimal.
- The denominator $I_0(\beta)$ normalises the window so that its peak value (at $n = N/2$) is **unity**.

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the empirical formula for the filter order $N$ required to meet specifications $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$, $\delta_p$, $\delta_s$ when using a Kaiser window. Explain the physical meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a lowpass FIR filter, $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample, $\omega_s = 0.35\pi$ rad/sample, $\delta_p = 0.05$, $\delta_s = 0.01$. Compute the Kaiser window parameters $\beta$, $N$, and write the first three and last three samples of the window. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The derivation begins with Kaiser's empirical relationship between the **stopband attenuation $A$** (in dB), the **transition width $\Delta\omega$**, and the **filter order $N$**. Kaiser fitted this to thousands of DPSS-based optimal designs and obtained:

$$
N \approx \frac{A - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega}
$$

**Physical meaning of each term:**

- $A = -20 \log_{10}\!\left[\min(\delta_p, \delta_s)\right]$ — the **minimum stopband attenuation** required (in dB). Higher $A \Rightarrow$ harder spec $\Rightarrow$ larger $N$.
- $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ — the **transition bandwidth** (in rad/sample). Smaller $\Delta\omega$ $\Rightarrow$ sharper filter $\Rightarrow$ larger $N$.
- $2.285$ — empirical slope coefficient; it is the reciprocal of the *DPSS main-lobe width* normalised to one radian.
- $8$ — empirical offset accounting for the *fixed* main-lobe energy (independent of $A$).
- The final $N$ is the **ceiling** of the ratio, ensuring the smallest integer meeting the spec.

The order $N$ grows *linearly* with $A$ and *inversely* with $\Delta\omega$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Attenuation $A$:**

$$
A = -20 \log_{10}[\min(0.05, 0.01)] = -20 \log_{10}(0.01) = 40 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Since $21 \le A = 40 \le 50$, use the mid-range formula for $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = 0.5842\,(A - 21)^{0.4} + 0.07886\,(A - 21)
$$

$$
A - 21 = 40 - 21 = 19
$$

$$
19^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 19} = e^{0.4 \times 2.9444} = e^{1.1778} = 3.247
$$

$$
\beta = 0.5842 \times 3.247 + 0.07886 \times 19 = 1.8970 + 1.4983 = 3.395
$$

**Step 3 — Transition width $\Delta\omega$:**

$$
\Delta\omega = 0.35\pi - 0.2\pi = 0.15\pi \text{ rad/sample}
$$

**Step 4 — Filter order $N$:**

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{A - 8}{2.285\,\Delta\omega} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{40 - 8}{2.285 \times 0.15\pi} \right\rceil
$$

Numerator: $40 - 8 = 32$.

Denominator: $2.285 \times 0.15\pi = 2.285 \times 0.4712 = 1.0767$.

Ratio: $32 / 1.0767 = 29.72$.

$$
N = \lceil 29.72 \rceil = 30
$$

Window length: $L = N + 1 = 31$ samples.

**Step 5 — Compute first three and last three samples (by symmetry $w[n] = w[N-n]$, with $N/2 = 15$):**

The index term for general $n$:

$$
\alpha_n = \left(\frac{n - 15}{15}\right)^{\!2}
$$

For $n = 0$: $\alpha_0 = (0-15/15)^2 = 1$, so $w[0] = I_0(\beta \cdot 0)/I_0(\beta) = 1/I_0(3.395)$.

$I_0(3.395) \approx 1 + (3.395/2)^2 + (3.395/4)^4/2!^2 + \cdots \approx 1 + 2.882 + 2.075 + 0.624 + 0.116 \approx 6.756$.

$$
w[0] = 1/6.756 \approx 0.1480
$$

For $n = 1$: $\alpha_1 = (1-15/15)^2 = (14/15)^2 = 0.8711$. $\sqrt{1 - 0.8711} = \sqrt{0.1289} = 0.3590$.

Argument: $3.395 \times 0.3590 = 1.2188$. $I_0(1.2188) \approx 1 + 0.3713 + 0.0345 + 0.0016 = 1.4085$.

$$
w[1] = 1.4085 / 6.756 \approx 0.2085
$$

For $n = 2$: $\alpha_2 = (2-15/15)^2 = (13/15)^2 = 0.7511$. $\sqrt{1 - 0.7511} = \sqrt{0.2489} = 0.4989$.

Argument: $3.395 \times 0.4989 = 1.6939$. $I_0(1.6939) \approx 1 + 0.7182 + 0.1289 + 0.0116 = 1.8628$.

$$
w[2] = 1.8628 / 6.756 \approx 0.2757
$$

By symmetry, $w[30] = w[0] \approx 0.1480$, $w[29] = w[1] \approx 0.2085$, $w[28] = w[2] \approx 0.2757$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to design an FIR lowpass filter using the Kaiser window. List the MATLAB (or equivalent) commands for each step and describe the role of the `kaiserord` function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using MATLAB/Python, a student designs a bandpass FIR filter with passband $0.3\pi$ to $0.6\pi$, stopband ripples $\delta_s = 0.001$ on both sides, transition widths $\Delta\omega = 0.05\pi$ on both sides. Calculate the required $A$, $\beta$, and $N$. Write the pseudo-code (or list of function calls) that would implement this design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step-by-step procedure for FIR lowpass filter design using Kaiser window:**

**Step 1 — Convert specifications to digital domain.** Given $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$, $\delta_p$, $\delta_s$ (all in rad/sample or Hz).

**Step 2 — Compute stopband attenuation $A$ (dB):**

$$
A = -20 \log_{10}[\min(\delta_p, \delta_s)]
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\beta$ using the appropriate Kaiser empirical formula** (based on whether $A$ is in the low, mid, or high regime).

**Step 4 — Compute transition width $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$.**

**Step 5 — Compute filter order $N$ using $N = \lceil (A-8)/(2.285\,\Delta\omega) \rceil$.**

**Step 6 — Generate the Kaiser window vector** $w[0], w[1], \ldots, w[N]$ using the Bessel-function formula.

**Step 7 — Compute the ideal lowpass impulse response:**

$$
h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - N/2))}{\pi (n - N/2)}, \quad \omega_c = (\omega_p + \omega_s)/2
$$

with $h_d[N/2] = \omega_c/\pi$ at the centre sample.

**Step 8 — Multiply:** $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$.

**Step 9 — Verify** using `freqz` and confirm the actual $A$ and $\Delta\omega$ meet the spec.

**MATLAB commands:**

- `Wp = 0.3*pi; Ws = 0.5*pi; Rp = 0.1; Rs = 60;` (define specs)
- `[N, Wn, beta, ftype] = kaiserord([Wp Ws]/(pi), [1 0], [10^(-Rp/20) 10^(-Rs/20)]);` — `kaiserord` is a MATLAB helper that **automatically computes $N$, $\beta$, and the normalised cutoff $W_n$** from the ripple and transition-width specs.
- `b = fir1(N, Wn, ftype, kaiser(N+1, beta));` — generates the FIR coefficients.
- `fvtool(b);` — visualises magnitude and phase response.

**Role of `kaiserord`:** It encapsulates steps 2–5 above into a single call, eliminating manual computation of $\beta$ and $N$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Attenuation $A$:**

The stopband ripple is $\delta_s = 0.001$, so:

$$
A = -20 \log_{10}(0.001) = -20 \times (-3) = 60 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Shape parameter $\beta$:**

Since $A = 60 > 50$, use the high-attenuation formula:

$$
\beta = 0.1102\,(A - 8.7) = 0.1102 \times (60 - 8.7) = 0.1102 \times 51.3 = 5.654
$$

**Step 3 — Filter order $N$:**

Transition width: $\Delta\omega = 0.05\pi$ rad/sample.

$$
N = \left\lceil \frac{60 - 8}{2.285 \times 0.05\pi} \right\rceil
$$

Numerator: $52$.

Denominator: $2.285 \times 0.05\pi = 2.285 \times 0.1571 = 0.3590$.

Ratio: $52 / 0.3590 = 144.85$.

$$
N = \lceil 144.85 \rceil = 145
$$

**Step 4 — Pseudo-code / function calls:**

**MATLAB version:**

```matlab
Wp = [0.3, 0.6];            % passband edges (normalised, fraction of pi)
Ws = [0.25, 0.65];          % stopband edges
Rp = 0.1;                   % passband ripple in dB (assumed; not in spec, use default 1 dB if unknown)
Rs = 60;                    % stopband attenuation in dB (= A)
[N, Wn, beta, ftype] = kaiserord([Wp Ws], [0 1 0], [10^(-Rs/20) 10^(-Rs/20)]);
b = fir1(N, Wn, ftype, kaiser(N+1, beta));
freqz(b, 1, 1024);
```

**Python (SciPy) version:**

```python
from scipy.signal import kaiserord, firwin, freqz
import numpy as np

A = 60                          # dB
width = 0.05                    # transition width (fraction of pi, normalised to Nyquist = 1)
numtaps, beta = kaiserord(A, width)
taps = firwin(numtaps, [0.3, 0.6], window=('kaiser', beta), pass_zero=False)
w, h = freqz(taps, worN=1024)
```

(7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Kaiser-Window Questions:**
> 1. **Sign error in $A$:** Students often write $A = 20 \log_{10}(\delta)$ (positive log) instead of $A = -20 \log_{10}(\delta)$. Result: $A$ becomes *negative*, leading to *invalid* $\beta$. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Wrong regime of $\beta$ formula:** Using the mid-range formula ($21 \le A \le 50$) when $A > 50$ is the most frequent error. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling operator:** Writing $N = (A-8)/(2.285\,\Delta\omega)$ as a *float* instead of rounding up. The actual filter would then have one tap too few and fail the spec. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting that $N+1$ is the window length, not $N$.** Many students write the window as $w[0..N-1]$ of length $N$ but the formulas assume length $N+1$. **Lose 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$ with normalised $W_n = \omega/\pi$ in MATLAB/Python.** When calling `fir1`, the cutoff $W_n$ must be **in the range [0, 1]**, *not* in [0, $\pi$]. **Lose 1 mark** in the software-tool question.
> 6. **Forgetting the special case $\beta = 0$ for $A < 21$ dB.** The formula only applies for $A \ge 21$. Below this, you must explicitly set $\beta = 0$, not plug a negative value into the mid-range formula. **Lose 0.5 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Kaiser window is the *only* adjustable window in KTU Module 4.** It is the **default choice** for FIR filter design in MATLAB's `fir1` and SciPy's `firwin`.
- The window formula is $w[n] = I_0\!\left[\beta\sqrt{1 - ((n-N/2)/(N/2))^2}\right]/I_0(\beta)$, normalised so that $w[N/2] = 1$ and $w[0] = w[N] = 1/I_0(\beta)$.
- The **modified Bessel function $I_0(x)$** is positive, even, and monotonically increasing. Series: $1 + (x/2)^2 + (x/2)^4/(2!)^2 + \cdots$.
- **$\beta$ is the *shape parameter***: $\beta = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ Rectangular; $\beta \approx 4.55$ $\Rightarrow$ Hamming-like; $\beta \approx 8$ $\Rightarrow$ Blackman-like.
- **Three-step design recipe:** (i) $A = -20\log_{10}[\min(\delta_p,\delta_s)]$, (ii) $\beta$ from the empirical formula, (iii) $N = \lceil (A-8)/(2.285\,\Delta\omega) \rceil$.
- **Two regimes of $\beta$ formula:** $A > 50$ uses the simple linear formula; $21 \le A \le 50$ uses the nonlinear $(A-21)^{0.4}$ form; $A < 21$ sets $\beta = 0$.
- **Filter coefficients:** $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$, where $h_d[n]$ is the ideal (sinc) impulse response.
- **MATLAB helpers:** `kaiserord` (computes $N, \beta, W_n$) and `fir1(N, Wn, ftype, kaiser(N+1, beta))` (generates coefficients).
- **Python helper:** `scipy.signal.kaiserord(ripple_dB, width_normalised)` returns `(numtaps, beta)`.
- **Trade-off rule of thumb:** doubling $A$ (from 40 to 80 dB) approximately **doubles** the required filter order $N$, all else being equal.
- **Stopband attenuation is positive dB** — never negative in the design formulas; the negative sign is in the $\log_{10}$ of $\delta < 1$.
- **Window length is $N+1$,** filter order is $N$ — a frequent source of off-by-one errors.
